results.content:
Once a traditional wet market, it now boasts a modern atmosphere filled with local concept stores, design boutiques, rotating exhibitions, and a variety of dining options. While it has undergone renovations, the original architectural features—such as tiled floors, steel staircases, and open courtyards—are still prominent. It’s an ideal destination for enjoying a coffee, exploring unique finds, and discovering local designers.

### 6) Temple Street Night Market in Kowloon

Temple Street is one of Hong Kong’s most iconic night markets, especially lively in the evenings with street food, fortune tellers, and vendors. One of Hong Kong’s most atmospheric and authentic experiences, Temple Street offers a vibrant snapshot of the city after dark. [...] This historic street, which runs through Yau Ma Tei and Mong Kok in Kowloon, is home to long-established shops offering a variety of specialty kitchen items, porcelain, and unique knick-knacks.

You can discover an impressive range of products, including razor-sharp cleavers, bamboo steamers, cast-iron woks, chopping boards, and dim sum baskets. The options are vast and include specialty tools for baking and candy-making, as well as large industrial mixers. [...] Food and scenery are standout reasons to visit Lamma Island. The island is famous for its waterfront seafood restaurants, where you can enjoy fresh fish, prawns, and classic Cantonese dishes with sea breezes and sunset views. Beyond dining, Lamma’s walking trails lead to quiet beaches, hidden coves, and panoramic lookouts perfect for hiking and swimming. Whether you’re looking for a relaxing day trip, a nature-filled adventure, or a long lunch by the water, Lamma Island delivers a memorable and beautifully balanced Hong Kong experience.

### 8) Drinks At The Ritz-Carlton (Highest Bar In The World)

Last Updated on April 15, 2026

# THE ESSENTIAL HONG KONG TRAVEL GUIDE (2026)

*This post may contain affiliate links. As an Amazon Associate I earn from qualifying purchases. We may receive a small commission when you make a purchase using our link.*

![Hong Kong Travel Guide](https://cdn.wearetravelgirls.com/spai/q_glossy+ret_img+to_webp/wearetravelgirls.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/Hong-Kong-Travel-Guide-222x222.jpg)

Hong Kong is a vibrant metropolis where East meets West, blending centuries-old Chinese traditions with a distinctly modern, international edge. Officially a Special Administrative Region of China, Hong Kong sits on the southern coast of the country, facing the South China Sea. Made up of Hong Kong Island, the Kowloon Peninsula, the New Territories, and more than 200 outlying islands, the city is famous for its dramatic skyline, bustling harbors, and surprising pockets of nature just minutes from the urban core.

Home to around 7.5 million people, Hong Kong is one of the most densely populated places in the world, yet it remains remarkably efficient, clean, and easy to navigate. Cantonese and English are the official languages, and the city’s history as a former British colony has shaped everything from its legal system to its food scene. This cultural duality creates a place where traditional temples stand beside sleek skyscrapers, and centuries-old customs coexist with cutting-edge innovation.

What truly sets [Hong Kong](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/index.html) apart is its unmatched energy and diversity. It’s a global financial hub and shopping destination, yet also a paradise for food lovers, offering everything from Michelin-starred restaurants to legendary street food and dim sum halls. Beyond the city streets, visitors can hike lush mountain trails, relax on sandy beaches, or explore fishing villages that feel worlds away from modern city life.

For travelers exploring Asia, Hong Kong is a must visit destination that offers an incredible introduction to the region’s contrasts. It’s a city that feels both deeply Chinese and unmistakably global. Whether you’re visiting for a quick stopover or a  longer getaway, this is guide will help you plan an unforgettable trip to Hong Kong.

![Vanessa Rivers, Hong Kong Views](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Vanessa Rivers, Hong Kong Views](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Where I Stayed In Hong Kong: Upper House

During my trip to Hong Kong, I stayed at [Upper House](https://www.upperhouse.com/en/hongkong/), and fell in love with every aspect of this magical, “home away from home.” Upper House Hong Kong is far more than just a place to sleep; it’s a deeply considered experience that feels like staying in a beautiful, thoughtfully designed home rather than a typical hotel.

From the moment you arrive, the philosophy of **“Houses Not Hotels”** is evident. Upper House strives to feel like a contemporary private residence in the sky, with bespoke service that are attentive without being intrusive, and spaces that feel warm, calm, and personal. Each stay is shaped by the people and experiences that make the house feel uniquely yours, making it more like a retreat than a transient stopover.  

![Upper House Hong Kong Views](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Upper House Hong Kong Views](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

I arrived in my beautiful suite to find personalized gifts; silky soft pajamas to take home, a clutch with my initials on it, chocolates, wellness drinks, a welcome home sign, and the most amazing views of Hong Kong Island from every window.

The **location in Admiralty**, perched above Pacific Place and connected directly to one of Hong Kong’s premier shopping and dining complexes, couldn’t be more convenient. When you stay at [Upper House](https://www.upperhouse.com/en/hongkong/), you’re within easy reach of Central, the Star Ferry, the Peak Tram, and numerous cultural attractions. Yet high above the bustle, here you’ll find an elevated sense of calm and quiet that’s rare in the city.  

![Upper House Hong Kong Views](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Upper House Hong Kong Views](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

One of Upper House’s biggest draws is its **breathtaking views**. Every room and suite offers panoramic vistas of Victoria Harbour, the skyline, or the verdant peaks surrounding the city. Floor-to-ceiling windows and thoughtful design make the cityscape part of the experience — whether you’re watching ferries glide across the water at sunset or seeing skyscrapers twinkle at night.

The **suites and rooms** themselves are a highlight: spacious, elegantly appointed, and furnished with quality materials and comforts that make them feel like your own urban sanctuary. Many feature oversized bathtubs beside picture windows, generous living spaces, and curated art and design elements that elevate the experience of simply being in your room.

![Upper House Hong Kong Suite](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Upper House Hong Kong Suite](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[Dining at Upper House](https://www.upperhouse.com/en/hongkong/restaurants-and-bars/) is equally exceptional, with venues like **Salisterra**, the Sky Lounge, The Continental, The Lawn, and The Green Room offering everything from refined Mediterranean cuisine to intimate cocktails with a view. These spaces are designed to be social and relaxed while delivering exceptional culinary experiences.

I was treated to an amazing tasting menu during my stay, and each offering was fantastic. The delicious dishes, paired with excellent champagne and wine, plus amazing views, created an all-around exquisite dining experience.

![Upper House Hong Kong Dining](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Upper House Hong Kong Dining](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Upper House also places strong emphasis on **sustainability and wellbeing**. The property actively works to reduce waste by eliminating single-use plastics, offering filtered water systems to cut down on bottles, and participating in community and recycling programs.

Their [wellness offerings](https://www.upperhouse.com/en/hongkong/wellness/wellness-residencies/) are also unique and expansive, from yoga and meditation sessions to curated wellness residencies and in-room treatments — all designed to nourish both body and mind in a city known for its high pace.  

In short, staying at Upper House means enjoying luxury that feels personal, experiences that feel intentional, and a sense of place that feels like a beautiful home perched above one of the most dynamic cities in the world.

[BOOK YOUR STAY AT UPPER HOUSE](https://www.upperhouse.com/en/hongkong/)

![Vanessa Rivers, Upper House Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Vanessa Rivers, Upper House Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## 12 Of The Best Hotels In Hong Kong

If you get the chance, I highly suggest staying at [Upper House Hong Kong.](https://www.upperhouse.com/en/hongkong/) But they only have 117 rooms, so if you find they are sold out, not to worry. Hong Kong offers a wide range of upscale hotels to suit every preference and budget. Below, I’m sharing 12 of the best hotels in Hong Kong.

### 1) Four Seasons Hotel Hong Kong

This hotel adds a lively atmosphere to the city, offering newly refurbished rooms with spectacular harbor views and innovative lighting from the ICC. Guests can experience top-notch service and a range of excellent amenities, including a spa with 17 treatment rooms, some designed specifically for couples. Additionally, the hotel features 10 restaurants and two swimming pools.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.kqzyfj.com/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-Four-Seasons-Hotel-Hong-Kong.h1418778.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/four-seasons-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 2) Conrad Hong Kong

This exquisite hotel combines tranquility with a convenient location, providing breathtaking panoramic views of the city and harbor from its roomy, elegantly appointed accommodations.

Guests can savor delicious Italian and Chinese dishes, a wide selection of breakfast items, and outstanding service. The hotel also features five dining options, an on-site shopping center, an outdoor swimming pool, and a relaxing spa.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.dpbolvw.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-Conrad-Hong-Kong.h16639.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/conrad-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 3) The Peninsula Hong Kong

Nestled in the Peninsula area, this elegant property offers a tranquil and historic ambiance, providing a timeless experience for guests. You can relish high tea with live music, unwind at the luxurious spa, and enjoy stunning views of Victoria Harbour from the pool deck.

This upscale hotel boasts seven restaurants, a full-service spa, and an indoor swimming pool. With more than 300 rooms available, some provide picturesque harbour views, and there are options that can accommodate up to four people.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.dpbolvw.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FKowloon-Hotels-The-Peninsula-Hong-Kong.h7056.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/the-peninsula-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 4) Mandarin Oriental, Hong Kong

This chic and lively urban retreat offers breathtaking views of Central Pier and the Ferris Wheel. Guests can experience outstanding service, elegant accommodations, and an executive lounge that adds a touch of luxury to their stay.

Situated on Hong Kong Island, this upscale hotel is directly connected to a shopping center and just a short walk from the Legislative Council Building and the Landmark Shopping Area. The hotel offers a range of rooms with city, partial sea, and harbor views. There are various room types available, accommodating between one and three guests, including both standard rooms and suites.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.anrdoezrs.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-Mandarin-Oriental.h6095.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/mandarin-oriental-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 5) Lanson Place Causeway Bay

This luxury family-friendly hotel, newly renovated in 2024, offers a peaceful retreat close to Hong Kong Sogo. Guests can enjoy round-the-clock access to the gym, indulge in international cuisine at Salon Lanson, and take advantage of premium in-room features, including hydromassage showers and 55-inch Smart TVs.

Accommodation includes a selection of two-bedroom suites, one-bedroom suites, studios, a penthouse, and twin rooms. The largest suite comfortably accommodates up to four guests, featuring separate bedrooms and living areas.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.jdoqocy.com/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-Lanson-Place-Causeway-Bay.h1391380.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/lanson-place.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 6) The Murray, Hong Kong, a Niccolo Hotel

Trendy architectural design meets serene park views, just steps from Hong Kong’s vibrant Central district. Indulge in spacious, luxurious rooms, a rooftop bar with stunning vistas, and five exceptional dining options. There is also an outdoor swimming pool.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.tkqlhce.com/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-The-Murray.h20009998.Hotel-Information) |  [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/the-murray-hong-kong-a-niccolo.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 7) JW Marriott Hotel Hong Kong

Located close to the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre and Wan Chai Star Ferry Pier, JW Marriott Hotel Hong Kong offers a poolside bar and an on-site shopping mall. The hotel has an outdoor swimming pool, six on-site restaurants and a coffee shop.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.anrdoezrs.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-JW-Marriott-Hotel-Hong-Kong.h6792.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/jw-marriott-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 8) Island Shangri-La, Hong Kong

Situated in a prime location, this hotel boasts luxurious rooms with spectacular harbour views and direct access to Pacific Place. Guests can enjoy exquisite dining options and the ease of a limousine service, guaranteeing a smooth and unforgettable stay.

Island Shangri-La, Hong Kong, offers an elegant luxury experience with six unique restaurants featuring outstanding cuisine. The spa also offers rejuvenating body treatments and aromatherapy for a perfect escape.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.anrdoezrs.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-Island-Shangri-La.h14435.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/island-shangri-la-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 9) The St. Regis Hong Kong

The St. Regis Hong Kong presents a sophisticated ambiance and calming environments, serving as a serene escape within the bustling city. Visitors can take advantage of personalized room service and delightful touches, such as embroidered teddy bears. The hotel boasts a tranquil pool and gym, along with a steam room and outdoor pool for ultimate relaxation.

Additionally, guests have the opportunity to indulge in aromatherapy treatments, enjoy exquisite French cuisine at any of the three restaurants, or relax in the garden with complimentary newspapers at their leisure.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.anrdoezrs.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-The-St-Regis-Hong-Kong.h35555583.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/the-st-regis-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 10) Renaissance Hong Kong Harbour View Hotel

Nestled by Victoria Harbour, this elegant hotel boasts roomy, contemporary accommodations with spectacular views. Guests can enjoy a lovely pool, outstanding dining, and convenient transportation to nearby attractions.

Situated near Times Square Shopping Mall, the Renaissance Hong Kong Harbour View Hotel provides on-site shopping, a garden, and laundry/dry cleaning services. You can indulge in breakfast, lunch, and dinner at any of the three on-site restaurants, which serve a diverse array of international dishes.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.kqzyfj.com/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FHong-Kong-Hotels-Renaissance-Hong-Kong-Harbour-View-Hotel.h849905.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/renaissance-harbour-view-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 11) The Ritz-Carlton Hong Kong

Staying at The Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong is an unforgettable experience thanks to its location atop the International Commerce Centre, making it one of the highest hotels in the world. From the moment you arrive, you’re treated to breathtaking panoramic views of Victoria Harbour and the city skyline from nearly every room. The sleek, modern design, spacious accommodations, and floor-to-ceiling windows create a sense of luxury that feels both dramatic and serene.

Beyond the views, the hotel delivers exceptional service and world-class amenities. Guests can enjoy Michelin-starred dining, the iconic OZONE rooftop bar, and a stunning indoor infinity pool overlooking the city far below. With its prime location in Kowloon, seamless access to shopping and transport, and an atmosphere of refined elegance, The Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong offers a stay that feels truly elevated in every sense.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.dpbolvw.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FKowloon-Hotels-The-Ritz-Carlton-Hong-Kong.h4011564.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/the-ritz-carlton-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

### 12) Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui Hong Kong

During my trip, I also stayed at Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui Hong Kong and loved it. If it’s in your budget, I highly suggest splurging on a room with a plunge pool bathtub. These lovely soaking tubs fit two people, feature jets, and offer incredible views of Vitoria Harbor.

Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui Hong Kong also has a great bar area, a rooftop pool, and a central location. My only complaint is that the elevators were quite slow during my stay. However, they had just opened, so hopefully this issue has been resolved. All around, I had a wonderful stay here.

[BOOK NOW ON EXPEDIA](https://www.kqzyfj.com/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FKowloon-Hotels-Kimpton-Tsim-Sha-Tsui-Hong-Kong-By-Ihg.h118973468.Hotel-Information) | [BOOK NOW ON BOOKING.COM](https://www.booking.com/hotel/hk/kimpton-tsim-sha-tsui-hong-kong.en.html?aid=1435586&no_rooms=1&group_adults=2)

![Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Top 15 Things To Do In Hong Kong

Hong Kong is a city brimming with energy, giving it an atmosphere that never truly sleeps. With its bright lights and lively streets, there’s an abundance of things to explore.

One of the unique aspects of Hong Kong is that, even though some attractions may cater more to tourists than locals, you often find both groups enjoying the same places, trying the same restaurants, and strolling along the same routes.

This phenomenon reflects the city’s remarkable density and the principle that if something is working well, there’s no need for change. Whether it’s your first visit to Hong Kong or you’ve been here before, there’s always something new and exciting to discover and explore.

### 1) Take In The View From Victoria Peak

[Victoria Peak](https://www.thepeak.com.hk/en), commonly known as “The Peak,” is a must-see attraction during your first visit to Hong Kong. The optimal times to visit are either in the morning or at sunset, as both offer spectacular views.

From the top, you can look out over the city’s skyscrapers and take in the sight of Victoria Harbour, with Kowloon visible in the background. This view is one of Hong Kong’s most iconic scenes, and experiencing it firsthand is truly unforgettable.

The highest vantage point at Victoria Peak is the Sky Terrace 428, which sits at an elevation of 428 meters above sea level. Positioned atop the Peak Tower, it offers a 360-degree panoramic view of the city. Once you’ve soaked in the scenery, you can explore the pleasant walking paths and nearby cafés.

You can get to Victoria Peak by taxi, bus, or on foot, but riding the Peak Tram is a cool, classic experience. The tram ride lasts about six minutes and climbs steeply up the mountain, passing by towering skyscrapers. For the best views during the ride, try to sit at the front, on the right side of the tram.

![Vanessa Rivers, Victoria Peak, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Vanessa Rivers, Victoria Peak, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### 2) Watch Symphony Of Lights: Hong Kong’s Magical Light Show

Each evening at 8pm, the Hong Kong skyline comes alive with a captivating spectacle of lights and music, showcasing one of the largest light-and-sound shows in the world—all completely free of charge.

For 13 minutes, 44 iconic buildings on Hong Kong Island and in Kowloon coordinate dazzling light beams and animations set to music played through speakers.

### 3) Take The Star Ferry

One of the most iconic experiences in Hong Kong is riding the [Star Ferry](https://www.starferry.com.hk/en/home) across Victoria Harbour, which links Hong Kong Island and Kowloon. This classic activity is not only affordable, but also scenic and practical.

The Star Ferry has been serving passengers since 1888, and is arguably the most charming mode of transportation in the city. While modern metro trains speed beneath the harbor, and skyscrapers dominate the skyline, you can relax on a vintage green-and-white ferry.

The trip takes just eight to ten minutes and offers beautiful views of the harbor and city. For less than a dollar, you can enjoy stunning views—be sure to sit on the upper deck—as you make your way to the other side.

![Hong Kong Star Ferry](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Hong Kong Star Ferry](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### 4) Relax At Hong Kong Park

[Hong Kong Park](https://www.lcsd.gov.hk/en/parks/hkp/) is a serene oasis amid the bustling city. Despite being surrounded by towering skyscrapers, you’ll find it filled with the sounds of chirping birds and the gentle flow of water. It’s a perfect place to relax.

You can explore the Edward Youde Aviary, where tropical birds soar freely, the Forsgate Conservatory, which showcases cacti, palms, and vibrant exotic flowers, and the Museum of Tea Ware, set in an elegant colonial building. Or you can just wander the park and pet all the cats, which you’ll find around every turn.

### 5) Central Market

[Central Market](https://www.centralmarket.hk/) represents a captivating fusion of historical and contemporary elements. This Art Deco building, built in 1939, has been beautifully updated and repurposed as a lively creative venue.

Once a traditional wet market, it now boasts a modern atmosphere filled with local concept stores, design boutiques, rotating exhibitions, and a variety of dining options. While it has undergone renovations, the original architectural features—such as tiled floors, steel staircases, and open courtyards—are still prominent. It’s an ideal destination for enjoying a coffee, exploring unique finds, and discovering local designers.

### 6) Temple Street Night Market in Kowloon

Temple Street is one of Hong Kong’s most iconic night markets, especially lively in the evenings with street food, fortune tellers, and vendors. One of Hong Kong’s most atmospheric and authentic experiences, Temple Street offers a vibrant snapshot of the city after dark.

As the sun sets, the street comes alive with glowing lanterns, buzzing crowds, and the sounds of vendors calling out their offerings. It’s a place where tradition and everyday local life collide—street fortune tellers set up beside stalls selling antiques, souvenirs, electronics, and quirky curiosities you didn’t know you needed. 

Food is a major reason to visit Temple Street Night Market. The open-air dai pai dong–style food stalls serve up classic Cantonese favorites like clay pot rice, seafood, noodles, and sizzling street snacks, all best enjoyed at plastic tables under the neon lights. It’s casual, lively, and deeply local—perfect for sampling traditional dishes while people-watching late into the night. 

![Temple Street, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Temple Street, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### 7) Day Trip To Lamma Island

Lamma Island offers a refreshing escape from Hong Kong’s fast-paced urban energy, making it a perfect contrast to the city’s towering skyscrapers and bustling streets. Just a short ferry ride away, the island feels relaxed and laid-back, with car-free paths, colorful villages, and scenic coastal views. Visitors can wander through charming streets in Yung Shue Wan or Sok Kwu Wan, browse small boutiques, and enjoy a slower rhythm of life that showcases a different side of Hong Kong—one that’s creative, outdoorsy, and deeply connected to nature.

Food and scenery are standout reasons to visit Lamma Island. The island is famous for its waterfront seafood restaurants, where you can enjoy fresh fish, prawns, and classic Cantonese dishes with sea breezes and sunset views. Beyond dining, Lamma’s walking trails lead to quiet beaches, hidden coves, and panoramic lookouts perfect for hiking and swimming. Whether you’re looking for a relaxing day trip, a nature-filled adventure, or a long lunch by the water, Lamma Island delivers a memorable and beautifully balanced Hong Kong experience.

### 8) Drinks At The Ritz-Carlton (Highest Bar In The World)

The highest bar in the world is generally considered to be [Ozone](https://www.ozonebarhongkong.com), located on the 118th floor of [The Ritz-Carlton](https://www.dpbolvw.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FKowloon-Hotels-The-Ritz-Carlton-Hong-Kong.h4011564.Hotel-Information), Hong Kong. Offering stunning views from the International Commerce Centre (ICC), it’s known for its ethereal design, Asian tapas, and creative cocktails, providing an almost surreal experience high above the city’s skyline.

But in my opinion, [The Lounge & Bar](https://www.loungeandbarhongkong.com) at [The Ritz-Carlton](https://www.dpbolvw.net/click-8449023-11552045?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.expedia.com%2FKowloon-Hotels-The-Ritz-Carlton-Hong-Kong.h4011564.Hotel-Information) is far superior. Just a few floors down from Ozone, the lounge bar offers better cocktails and incredible views of Hong Kong Island, Victoria Harbor, and the islands beyond. This is the ultimate spot to grab a late-afternoon cocktail and a bite in Hong Kong.

![Ritz-Carlton Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Ritz-Carlton Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### 9) Day Trip To Macau

Taking a day trip to Macau from Hong Kong offers a fascinating blend of cultures you won’t find anywhere else in Asia. Just an hour away by [high-speed ferry](https://www.turbojet.com.hk/en/routing-sailing-schedule/hong-kong-macau/sailing-schedule-fares.aspx), Macau feels distinctly different, with its Portuguese colonial architecture, historic squares, and UNESCO-listed sites like the Ruins of St. Paul’s and Senado Square. Wandering through the old town reveals cobblestone streets, pastel-colored buildings, and centuries of history that showcase Macau’s unique East-meets-West heritage.

Beyond its historic charm, Macau is also known for its world-class dining, entertainment, and iconic skyline. From sampling famous Portuguese egg tarts and Macanese cuisine to visiting lavish resorts and casinos, the city packs a lot into a short visit. Whether you’re exploring ancient temples, enjoying waterfront views along the Cotai Strip, or simply soaking in the contrast to Hong Kong’s urban energy, Macau makes for a memorable and rewarding day trip.

![Macau](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Macau](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### 10) Walk Around Kowloon Walled City Park

[Kowloon Walled City Park](https://www.lcsd.gov.hk/tc/parks/kwcp/index.html) boasts an intriguing history. It was once one of the most densely populated regions in the world, marked by lax regulation, narrow alleyways, and unauthorized structures.

Today, however, it has been transformed into a beautifully landscaped garden that includes ponds, pavilions, and lush greenery. Visitors can still discover remnants of its past, including sections of the original city wall, ancient gates, and informative displays depicting life in the Walled City.

### 11) Shop For Antiques on Cat Street

For a glimpse of the quirky charm and nostalgia of old Hong Kong, head to Upper Lascar Row in Sheung Wan, commonly known as ‘Cat Street.’ This historic pedestrian street is filled with antique shops, vintage collectibles, old photographs, and unique trinkets.

Established in the early 20th century, Cat Street was once infamous for selling stolen goods, referred to as ‘mouse goods,’ along with other black-market items, all at prices much lower than those found in regular shops. Shoppers were often compared to cats hunting for mice, “catching” amazing deals, which inspired the name ‘Cat Street.’

Today, it serves as a treasure trove for antique lovers, showcasing rare music records, vintage clocks and cameras, and unique souvenirs. As you wander through the bustling stalls, quaint boutiques, and cozy cafes (be sure to try Halfway Coffee), you’ll find an array of items, including Mao-era propaganda posters, jade carvings, porcelain vases, Buddhist relics, old coins, calligraphy scrolls, and nostalgic movie posters.

### 12) Hike Dragon’s Back

Hiking [Dragon’s Back](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/us/explore/great-outdoor/dragon-s-back-a-scenic-hike-from-shek-o-to-big-wave-bay.html) is one of the best ways to experience Hong Kong’s natural beauty, offering a dramatic contrast to the city’s dense urban landscape. The trail is famous for its sweeping ridge-top views of the South China Sea, Shek O, and the surrounding islands. Accessible yet incredibly scenic, Dragon’s Back is suitable for most fitness levels and rewards hikers with panoramic vistas, ocean breezes, and lush greenery that make you forget you’re just minutes from skyscrapers.

Beyond the views, Dragon’s Back delivers a well-rounded outdoor experience that captures the adventurous side of Hong Kong. The trail is well-marked and social, often filled with locals and travelers enjoying the outdoors, especially on weekends.

You can easily pair the hike with a beach visit in Shek O, Big Wave Bay, or Repulse Bay afterward, turning it into a perfect half-day escape. For anyone wanting to see a different, more relaxed side of Hong Kong, hiking Dragon’s Back is a must.

![Dragon's Back Hong Kong Hike](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Dragon's Back Hong Kong Hike](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### 13) Beach Day at Repulse Bay

Repulse Bay is one of Hong Kong Island’s most iconic beaches, offering a rare blend of natural beauty and easy accessibility. Located on the southern side of Hong Kong Island, it sits just a short drive from Central and Admiralty, making it a favorite escape for both locals and visitors. Framed by crescent-shaped sands, calm waters, and luxury residential buildings rising behind the shore, Repulse Bay feels worlds away from the city’s skyscrapers while still being firmly within the island’s urban orbit.

What makes [Repulse Bay](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/us/explore/great-outdoor/wellness/repulse-bay.html) especially appealing is its laid-back, resort-like atmosphere. The wide, golden beach is ideal for swimming, sunbathing, and leisurely walks along the promenade, while the water is generally calmer than in other parts of the island. At the eastern end of the bay, the colorful Tin Hau Temple complex adds cultural interest, with statues and pavilions that are popular for photos and offer insight into local traditions tied to the sea.

Beyond the beach itself, Repulse Bay is also a dining and lifestyle destination. The historic Repulse Bay building overlooking the sand is home to cafés and restaurants with terrace seating and ocean views, perfect for a long lunch or sunset drink. Whether you’re looking for a relaxing beach day, scenic coastal views, or a glimpse of Hong Kong’s more tranquil side, Repulse Bay delivers an experience that balances elegance, culture, and natural charm.

![Repule Bay, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Repule Bay, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### 14) Explore Shanghai Street

Shanghai Street stands out as Hong Kong’s top spot for high-quality kitchenware, serving as a neighborhood department store designed for chefs and passionate home cooks.

This historic street, which runs through Yau Ma Tei and Mong Kok in Kowloon, is home to long-established shops offering a variety of specialty kitchen items, porcelain, and unique knick-knacks.

You can discover an impressive range of products, including razor-sharp cleavers, bamboo steamers, cast-iron woks, chopping boards, and dim sum baskets. The options are vast and include specialty tools for baking and candy-making, as well as large industrial mixers.

Many shops offer great deals. For instance, Man Kee Chopping Board is renowned for its durable, solid-wood chopping blocks, available in multiple sizes at reasonable prices. Bamboo steamers can be found for just a few dollars, and smaller items—such as dumpling presses, chopsticks, and baking molds—make excellent, affordable souvenirs.

### 15) Night Out In Central

Central on Hong Kong Island is one of the city’s most exciting places to go out at night, buzzing with energy long after the workday ends. By evening, the business district transforms into a lively social hub, with rooftop bars, hidden speakeasies, and stylish lounges filling with locals and travelers alike. **Lan Kwai Fong and SoHo** are at the heart of the action, offering everything from casual cocktail spots to high-end nightlife, all set against a dramatic backdrop of neon lights and towering skyscrapers.

What makes Central especially fun is the variety packed into a compact area. You can start the night with sunset drinks overlooking Victoria Harbour, move on to world-class international dining, and end dancing or bar-hopping just steps away. The mix of cultures, creative cocktails, and electric street atmosphere gives Central a cosmopolitan edge that feels distinctly Hong Kong. Whether you’re after an elegant evening or a spontaneous night out, Central delivers nonstop energy and great vibes.

![Night out in Central, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Night out in Central, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Top Tours In Hong Kong

There are a variety of great tours available in Hong Kong. A great way to see the city, take to the water, or learn something new is on an organized tour. Here are **nine great tours** you can take in the city and beyond.

![Repulse Bay, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Repulse Bay, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## The Best Restaurants In Hong Kong

For foodies, Hong Kong is a fantastic city. Hong Kong offers amazing dim sum, affordable Michelin-starred restaurants, and a wide range of trendy hotspots, as well as traditional Hong Kong cafes where time seems to stand still.

As you explore Hong Kong, you’ll find fantastic cafes and restaurants around every corner. Here are **15 top restaurant picks** in Hong Kong.

![One Harbour Road, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![One Harbour Road, Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## The Best Bars In Hong Kong

Hong Kong features a SoHo neighborhood, much like those in New York City and London. This lively area, situated south of Hollywood Road, offers a variety of trendy bars, restaurants, cultural attractions, and shopping venues.

Because it is close to the LKF party district, it is often a popular place to kick off an evening. Nonetheless, SoHo is renowned for its fantastic selection of watering holes. Here are 10 of the top bar picks in Hong Kong.

![Ritz Carlton Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Ritz Carlton Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## How To Get To Hong Kong

Hong Kong International Airport (HKG) is a central global hub, with daily direct flights from Vancouver, Auckland, Paris, London, Chicago, Dallas, Los Angeles, New York, Seattle and other major European and Asian destinations. It can also be accessed by intercity train or ferry from mainland China.

Upon arrival, you can use the Airport Express train, buses, taxis, or the MTR system to get around the city. There are tons of taxis available at the airport. Most taxi drivers in Hong Kong speak English, but they usually only accept cash, so be sure to get local currency (HKD) upon arrival.

![Hong Kong Airport](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Hong Kong Airport](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Best Time Of Year To Visit Hong Kong

The best time to visit Hong Kong is generally from **October to December**, when the weather is at its most pleasant. During these months, temperatures are comfortably mild, humidity drops significantly, and skies are often clear—ideal conditions for sightseeing, hiking, or enjoying Victoria Harbour. Autumn also brings cultural highlights, such as the Mid-Autumn Festival and various outdoor events, making it a lively yet comfortable season for exploring the city.

Another excellent period is **March to early May**, when spring arrives with warmer temperatures and blooming greenery. While humidity begins to rise in late spring, this season offers fewer crowds and better hotel rates than the peak autumn months. Travelers should be aware that June through September is hot and humid, with frequent typhoons, though it can appeal to bargain hunters, while January and February are cooler and can be busy due to Lunar New Year celebrations.

![Hong Kong Average weather](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Hong Kong Average weather](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Hong Kong Ultimate Travel Guide Wrap Up

With its rich culture, stunning scenery, and nonstop buzz, Hong Kong delivers an experience that is dynamic, unforgettable, and uniquely its own.

I hope this Hong Kong guide helps you plan an unforgettable trip to this incredible destination! Whether you’re heading to Asia for a [girls’ getaway](https://wearetravelgirls.com/greece-girls-getaways/), a couples vacation, or a family escape, Hong Kong is a city you don’t want to miss.

If you have questions about my stay at [Upper House](https://www.upperhouse.com/en/hongkong/), or my experience in Hong Kong, you can leave them in the comments below, or DM me [@VanessaRivers](https://www.instagram.com/vanessarivers/).

***We hope this article has inspired you to visit Hong Kong! If you have questions or tips to share with our readers, please comment below.***

results.content:
Statue Square, HSBC Lions, and Court of Final Appeal Cluster hiding in plain sight in Central: elegant square, iconic bronze HSBC lions (with rich feng shui and history), and grand neo-classical Court of Final Appeal building - very few independent tourists visit because wrong MTR exits lead elsewhere. So much layered colonial and modern history. Relevant: Peaceful square with water features and a lovely statue and great for people-watching, lion-rubbing tradition, and impressive architecture. How easy to visit: Central MTR (right exit is exit K)that) leads directly into Statue Square up an escalator, immediate area -flat, free, and seamless with any Central wander (pairs with St John's Cathedral) and you cannot miss HSBC which is basically attached to Statue Square and HSBS owns the land [...] The good news is that most of these 20 underrated spots are located in or very near areas rich with authentic local dining options, so you can easily pair a short visit with a meal or even weave it around a half-day food tour without wasting time on long transfers to many different attractions. Here’s how integration food” typically works in practice:

Central & Sheung Wan spots (St John's Cathedral, Statue Square/HSBC Lions/Court of Final Appeal, Sheung Wan Historic Neighborhood): These sit right in the heart of neighborhoods popular for small-group street food tours and traditional cha chaan teng (Hong Kong-style cafes). After a quick wander, step into nearby spots for egg tarts, wonton noodles, or congee - or join a guided food walk that already covers this zone. [...] Peak-area walks (Lugard Road Lookout, Victoria Peak Garden, Barker Road, Pinewood Battery): These are compact and rewarding in the morning or early afternoon. Many people finish with a meal back down in Central or head to nearby spots for afternoon tea at The Peninsula Hotel itself - its legendary lobby experience pairs beautifully with a light bite or full high tea if your schedule allows, for the Peak itself, we 2 large malls there are plenty of food options including Michelin starred restaurants like Maks Noodles.

## J3 Group Hong Kong | J3 Consultants Hong Kong | J3 Private Tours Hong Kong

# **Creating Memories That Will Last A Lifetime**

![TripAdvisor](https://www.tripadvisor.com/img/cdsi/img2/branding/tripadvisor_logo_transp_340x80-18034-2.png)

# Guided by Stories, Private Tours of Hong Kong with Jamie

# A Resident since 1972 - Quality Private Tours of Hong Kong

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/614ecc0753cc6b1b589c6477/d196a5e2-d63f-4e47-9c3f-cb4c31001301/My+12+Favourite+Hong+Kong+Images+%282%29.jpg)

**click on the image to enlarge**

© Copyright Acknowledged | All rights reserved | Image taken by Jamie

# **The Sik Sik Yuen Wong Tai Sin Temple | Kowloon** **| Hong Kong**

**20 Underrated Spots in Hong Kong for 2026: Realistic Picks for English-Speaking Visitors | Low-Crowds, High Value, and Time Friendly**

Perhaps the title of this blog post should have read : 2026 list of 20 not so hidden gems and secret spots in Hong Kong !

#### Let me be clear, I am NOT an **influencer on Instagram, travel blogger or travel writer** sponsered by the Hong Kong Tourism Board or other organisations, these influencers have their hotels, meals and travel expenses paid for plus whatever they can wrangle off the authorities and unlike influencers, travel bloggers and travel writers I actually know something about Hong Kong because I have lived here since January 2nd 1972 and I am a Private Tour Guide and guess what, I really do know the place.

After 16 years as a private tour guide in Hong Kong ( I did one year of research before getting my first tour in early 2011) and completing well over 2,360+ tours, I’ve learned one simple truth: true “hidden gems” and “secret spots” no longer really exist in Hong Kong in this age of social media and AI-generated itineraries. What does still exist, however, are underrated, low-crowd places that deliver genuine value, atmosphere, and history - especially for English-speaking visitors who are short on time and tired of unrealistic suggestions that waste hours on visiting these places for minimal payoff.

… and some of these underrated places are hiding in plain sight!

I should also point out that I can be pretty blunt in my assessments, I am perfectly aware that if 20 people went to one of these places then you are going to get 20 diverse opinions but I have an advantage over influencers, travel writers and bloggers and others of that ilk and that is I have lived here for over 50 years and have a local wife and we raised 3 boys here and I have completed over 2,360+ private tours in addition and going back to the early 1980’s I ran a consultancy company and taking my clients to places ot interest and such was part of what I did., no one is paying me to write these blog posts, you will get my opinion and it is very likely you may well disagree but you know what, I know for a fact that many visitors completely miss out on visiting Statue Square in Central District and walking over to HSBC headquarters and rubbing the bronze lions and yet these are 2 of most iconic things you can do in Hong Kong and they are literally in the heart of the Central Business District in Hong Kong! and did I mention there are 3 major historic buildings - The Court of Final Appeal, The Old French Mission Building and St. Johns Cathedral within a 1 - 5 minute walk from Statue Square.

…. and one of those little stories I have, there is a tree in Statue Square near the Mandarin Oriental Hotel Main Entrnace that is famous becauase John Lennon and David Bowie climbed it in 1977 and I have photographic proof! oh and from 1982 to 1985 Statue Square was home to the HSBC bronze lions whilst thei headquarter building was demolished and the current headquariters was rebuilt.

I have spent a couple of days reading and researching posts written by travel bloggers and travel writers and I came to the conclusion that many spots or “gems” are in remote locations, they sound cool but there are real issues with transportation and timing… and I should also point out that Mainland Chinese Tourists have an amazing gift of sniffing out new places and will go there en masse after seeing it on Chinese Social Media.

It is a simple fact of reality that most visitors who come to Hong Kong for whatever reason are here for for 1 - 2 days only, 3 if they can manage it, this means that time is a real consideration when it comes to planning itineraries for your stay and in a nutshell you are going to have real issues if you use AI as they either totally underestimate timing or overestimate timing, this is not an issue for private tour guides because we are in the streets dealing with reality and we know the transport systems

That’s why I’ve put together my practical 2026 list of 20 underrated spots in Hong Kong. These are places I regularly take my clients to, or that consistently impress independent travellers, because they offer real rewards without excessive travel time or disappointment. Whether you have just those 1 - 2 days in the city or a full week, these spots are accessible by public transport and refreshingly free of overwhelming crowds. No advance bookings required for most, no 4-hour round trips for a 20-minute stop — just honest, worthwhile experiences that let you feel more of the real Hong Kong.

Many visitors to Hong Kong come with **food** high on their agenda - whether that means booking a popular English-speaking food tour (dim sum tastings, street food crawls in Central/Sheung Wan or Kowloon neighborhoods are especially common), joining a private tasting experience, or simply carving out time for leisurely meals and street eats. That’s completely understandable in a city famous for its food scene, and it’s one reason I’ve kept this list deliberately flexible.

For the record I do not do food tours, never have and never will but at the bottom of this post are the contact details for 3 amazing food tour operators should you want to book one.

The good news is that most of these 20 underrated spots are located in or very near areas rich with authentic local dining options, so you can easily pair a short visit with a meal or even weave it around a half-day food tour without wasting time on long transfers to many different attractions. Here’s how integration food” typically works in practice:

**Central & Sheung Wan spots** (St John's Cathedral, Statue Square/HSBC Lions/Court of Final Appeal, Sheung Wan Historic Neighborhood): These sit right in the heart of neighborhoods popular for small-group street food tours and traditional cha chaan teng (Hong Kong-style cafes). After a quick wander, step into nearby spots for egg tarts, wonton noodles, or congee - or join a guided food walk that already covers this zone.

**Wan Chai & Causeway Bay** (Blue House & Pak Tai Temple, Noon Day Gun): Excellent for combining with local eats. Wan Chai has lively wet markets and dai pai dong-style options; Causeway Bay offers everything from milk tea and pineapple buns to wonton noodles. A short visit here leaves plenty of room for a relaxed lunch or to tag onto a food-focused afternoon, however bear in mind that the Noonday Gun goes off at noon which is very time specific!

**Peak-area walks** (Lugard Road Lookout, Victoria Peak Garden, Barker Road, Pinewood Battery): These are compact and rewarding in the morning or early afternoon. Many people finish with a meal back down in Central or head to nearby spots for afternoon tea at The Peninsula Hotel itself - its legendary lobby experience pairs beautifully with a light bite or full high tea if your schedule allows, for the Peak itself, we 2 large malls there are plenty of food options including Michelin starred restaurants like Maks Noodles.

**Kowloon side** (Sik Sik Yuen Wong Tai Sin Temple, Nan Lian Garden/Chi Lin Nunnery, Kowloon Walled City Park, Sham Shui Po & Mei Ho House, The Peninsula Hotel): Sham Shui Po stands out as a street food paradise - think handmade cheung fun (rice noodle rolls), cart noodles, egg waffles, and old-school noodle shops like Lau Sum Kee. It’s easy to spend time at the Mei Ho House exhibition and then dive straight into local eats on Apliu Street or nearby alleys. Wong Tai Sin and the gardens combine well with a quick Kowloon-style lunch nearby - specifically there is a great veggie restaurant in the Nan Lian Garden while the Peninsula’s lobby makes a graceful end to a Tsim Sha Tsui food day.

**North Point & tram ride**: The iconic tram journey itself often passes lively markets - perfect for hopping off and grabbing fresh street snacks or egg waffles before or after exploring, North Point is a lively spot with street markets but it is the tram ride (upperdeck) that is iconic!\

**Outlying islands & hike** (Peng Chau, Cheung Chau, Mui Wo, Dragon’s Back): These naturally lend themselves to slower-paced days with built-in seafood or beachside meals. Ferries align well with lunch timing, so you can enjoy the spot and then sit down for fresh catches or casual waterfront dining without rushing. I love Cheung Chau Island for food, a lot of options and they are used to dealing with foreign tourists.

The key is to treat the list as a menu of flexible add-ons rather than a fixed itinerary. If you’ve booked a 2 - 3 hour food tour in the morning, pick one or two nearby spots for the afternoon (or vice versa). On shorter trips, one well-chosen spot plus a meal can easily fill half a day meaningfully. This way you avoid the common trap of overpacking and still get that satisfying mix of culture, views, history - and, of course, great Hong Kong food.

I should point out that on my private tours, most of my guests would prefer to explore rather than eat a meal or snack!

# **20 Hidden Gems + Secret Spots | Everywhere** **| Hong Kong**

Here’s my list (in no particular order beyond starting with one of my longtime favorites)

**Lugard Road Lookout (Victoria Peak) I have been to this spot over 6.000 times since 1972 -** One of the world's great panoramic city views after well over 100 years, yet it remains remarkably under-visited - most people stop at the crowded Peak Tower or the Lions Pavilion and miss this peaceful, immersive alternative. The sweeping harboor and skyline panorama feels far more special and serene here. Relevant: Flat, shaded paved path through mature forest with dramatic tree canopies and flexible timing for sunrise, day, or sunset. How easy to visit: Extremely easy - Peak Tram or bus #15 to the Peak and the most efficient way, take a taxi!, then a gentle 20-30 minute flat (ish) walk; suitable for all fitness levels with no planning needed. - on Tripadvisor it has only garnered around 10 reviews over the years! which is remarkable given that it is a 180 degree view vs a 90 degree view at the other places and I have a go to blog post on how to get to the Lugard Road Lookout on my site menu

**Peng Chau Island** Tiny, car-free outlying island that feels like a genuine fishing village escape, with walking trails, small beaches, and heritage sites that rarely appear on foreign tourists' radars. Expats often call it a favorite relaxed secret for its slow pace. Relevant: Short family-friendly loops take in Lung Mo Temple, quirky rock formations, and old factory art spots—ideal for a no-rush day. How easy to visit: Very easy—30-40 minute ferry from Central Pier 6; everything flat and walkable in 1-2 hours once there., I have been there only 10 times in the past decade but I had some friends who ran a restaurant there and they recently sold it! it was a terrfic place for a drink and snack and I must point out that I always loved the ferry ride to get there

**Cheung Chau Island** Bone-shaped, car-free island with tranquil village streets, quiet beaches, and old temples that serve as a genuine city escape for locals but stay off most international itineraries. Recent English forums praise its unhurried charm. Relevant: Yuk Hui Temple, short hilltop deck with sea views, and seafood spots along narrow lanes - great for casual wandering or renting a bike. How easy to visit: Straightforward 35-60 minute ferry from Central Pier 5; compact and mostly flat for easy exploration and boy, I would do this just for the ferry ride on the upper deck outdoor section! one downside, no cars but they have these village vehicles which are noisy motorised carts that are terribly noisy and irritate everybody!

**Mui Wo (Lantau Island)** Currently undergoing major building works near the ferry pier, Laid-back seaside village with waterfront calm, Silver Mine Bay Beach, and nearby waterfall that offers a refreshing contrast to busier Lantau areas. Expats highlight its relaxed, farm-to-table feel as a true urban antidote. Relevant: Art workshops and a low-key beach scene without hype. How easy to visit: Simple 35-55 minute ferry from Central Pier 6; key areas covered by short walks or local transport. Not many people visit these days, preferring to go to Tai O Fishing Village and the Big Buddha

**Nan Lian Garden** Classical Tang-dynasty-style oasis with ponds, vermillion (orange) bridges, and pavilions that magically feels removed from urban Kowloon. Often praised in hidden-gem roundups for its serene, contemplative quality. Relevant: Perfect for quiet reflection or photos; pairs naturally with the adjacent nunnery. How easy to visit: Extremely easy - MTR Tuen Ma Line to Diamond Hill Station, 9 minute walk; free and open daily and I have a real connection to the Nan Lian Garden and Chi Lin Nunnery, but that is a conversation for another day!

**Chi Lin Nunnery** Peaceful timber-framed Buddhist complex emphasizing silence and traditional architecture as a genuine urban sanctuary. Travellers note its respectful, non-touristy atmosphere. Relevant: Keep visits quiet for full immersion - contemplative and refreshing. How easy to visit: Same access as Nan Lian Garden (MTR Diamond Hill + short walk); free and yes there are about 60 nuns there but seeing one is like the sighting of Big Foot!

**Dragon's Back Hike** I do not do hiking tours in any shape or form but I have a friend Sabrina of Hong Kong Trails and Tours that does - Scenic ridge trail with sweeping sea and island views, often labeled one of the best accessible urban hikes - locals use it to unwind, keeping it blissfully uncrowded for most visitors. Hiking chats love the payoff versus effort. Relevant: Ends at Big Wave Bay beach for a refreshing cool-down; varied but rewarding terrain. How easy to visit: Bus #9 from Shau Kei Wan MTR/tram to trailhead; 2-3 hour moderate hike (well-marked), then easy bus/minibus return and yet I have only done it once in my life

**Pinewood Battery (near Victoria Peak)** Forgotten WWII-era gun battery and bunkers on a Peak spur with heritage info and elevated views - a quiet historical detour that few reach even when at the Peak. Heritage walkers flag it as a true secret. Relevant: Signed trail through bushland with old fortifications. How easy to visit: From the Peak (combine with Lugard), short signed walk via Morning Trail/Hatton Road; easy-moderate and free. it is roughly a 15 minute walk from the Lugard Road Lookout at Victoria Peak and there are signposts to guide you, I have been there about 10 times with history buffs

**Sheung Wan Historic Neighborhood** Backstreet district with temples, Dr. Sun Yat-sen Trail plaques, and everyday eateries that feel like stepping into older Hong Kong - deeper lanes rarely fully explored despite central location. English bloggers call it a neighborhood gem. Relevant: Man Mo Temple area plus quiet heritage for reflective wandering. How easy to visit: MTR to Sheung Wan Station; compact, flat, and fully walkable - ideal half-day. The Man Mo Temple is pretty iconic but Jammed with Mainland Chinese Tourists and others on cheap package tours from opening to closing so I avoid it these days and I do love the snake shop in Sheung Wan, lots of very poisonour snakes in cages ready to be made into snake soup

**St John's Cathedral** Hong Kong's oldest Anglican church (1849), a serene Gothic Revival escape hiding in plain sight amid Central's skyscrapers - most people have no idea it exists. English-speaking visitors appreciate the colonial history and peaceful garden. Relevant: Stained glass, wicker pews, and outdoor monuments as well as the WW2 section offer a calm historical breather; often paired with nearby Old French Mission Building or Henderson/Cheung Kong views on Garden Road. How easy to visit: Extremely easy -short walk from Central MTR (Garden Road area) or while heading to/from the Peak Tram; free, open daily, quick 15-30 minute stop and it has a unique claim to fame, they are the only “people” so to speak that actually physically own the land the Church is built on, everyone else has a land lease.

**Victoria Peak Garden** Lush Victorian-style garden on the former governor's summer home site, offering serene landscaping and alternative panoramic views (including south-side Aberdeen and the South China sea) that most Peak visitors miss entirely. It speaks for itself as a peaceful, under-visited retreat. Relevant: Pavilions, lawns, and fresh air away from the main crowds. How easy to visit: From Peak Tower/Galleria, 40 - 60 minute steady uphill walk via Mount Austin Road (or signed paths); combine with Lugard for a fuller Peak experience - rewarding with minimal extra effort and I have a go to blog post on how to get to Victoria Peak Garden on my site menu and ignore any nonsense from people who say it takes just 15 - 20 minutes to walk from the Peak Tower to Victoria Peak Garden, it is a very steep and taxing uphill walk and even fit people will curse when it is hot and humid, I generally take a taxi up there and walk down which is so much easier

**Barker Road at Victoria Peak** Pleasant walk along "Billionaires' Row" with historic buildings, lovely scenery, and one of the finest city views - surprisingly overlooked even by many at the Peak. Relevant: Elegant residential stretch with dramatic skyline perspectives and shaded, scenic strolling. How easy to visit: From the Peak area (easy add-on to Lugard or Peak Garden), flat or gentle paved sections; straightforward public transport at the end of Barker Road, a most enjoyable walk and I have a go to blog post on how to get to Barker Road on my site menu

**North Point (via street tram and markets)** Lively local neighborhood with wet markets and street life that feels authentically Hong Kong, accessed via the iconic double-decker tram ride (itself a highlight). Often skipped by tourists but loved by those seeking real everyday vibes. Relevant: Chun Yeung Street market with trams running through stalls, fresh produce, and old-school energy - great for photos and immersion. How easy to visit: Tram from Central/Wan Chai (scenic and fun) or direct MTR to North Point; compact area for easy wandering and easy to get back into town

**Blue House and Pak Tai Temple in Wan Chai** Striking blue heritage tenement cluster (1920s Lingnan style) and adjacent historic Taoist temple (1862, with impressive copper statue) that amazingly see few visitors despite their charm. Part of Wan Chai's quieter heritage side. Relevant: Colorful balconies, courtyard feel at the Blue House, and serene temple atmosphere—authentic cultural pocket. How easy to visit: MTR to Wan Chai, short walk (5-10 minutes from station); quick, flat, and free - fits neatly into a Wan Chai stroll. You have to walk through a market to get there and the Temple is a 2 minute walk from the Blue House on Lung On Street

**Noon Day Gun in Causeway Bay** Quirky waterfront tradition (daily noon firing since the 1860s, tied to Jardine Matheson history) that people talk about but hardly anyone actually goes to see - still surprising after all these years. Relevant: Short, fun ceremonial moment with colonial backstory; great for a timed, memorable stop. How easy to visit: MTR to Causeway Bay (Exit D1), 10-15 minute walk through underpass/tunnel to the site; time for noon (arrive early), otherwise quick exterior view - low effort if planned around lunch but is very time specific 12pm on the dot so you cannot be late!

**Statue Square, HSBC Lions, and Court of Final Appeal** Cluster hiding in plain sight in Central: elegant square, iconic bronze HSBC lions (with rich feng shui and history), and grand neo-classical Court of Final Appeal building - very few independent tourists visit because wrong MTR exits lead elsewhere. So much layered colonial and modern history. Relevant: Peaceful square with water features and a lovely statue and great for people-watching, lion-rubbing tradition, and impressive architecture. How easy to visit: Central MTR **(right exit is exit K)**that) leads directly into Statue Square up an escalator, immediate area -flat, free, and seamless with any Central wander (pairs with St John's Cathedral) and you cannot miss HSBC which is basically attached to Statue Square and HSBS owns the land lease for Statue Square

**Sik Sik Yuen Wong Tai Sin Temple** Iconic, photogenic Taoist temple (1921) that's incredibly ornate and interesting, yet rarely seen by foreign tourists unless on private tours - the exception that proves the rule for its appeal. Easy access and highly rewarding. Relevant: Vibrant halls, fortune-telling, and gardens; photogenic and culturally deep without feeling commercial. How easy to visit: Direct MTR to Wong Tai Sin Station (Exit B2/B3), 3-minute walk; free entry, open daily—can combine with Nan Lian Garden/Chi Lin Nunnery (one stop away on MTR) and it has always been one of my favourite places to visit despite the crowds, I have been well over a thousand times

**Kowloon Walled City Park** Tranquil green space with remnants of the once-lawless Walled City, now an educational historical oasis that tells a unique Hong Kong story. Frequently called an underrated heritage gem by history enthusiasts. Relevant: Ancient walls, ruins, and panels make it quietly fascinating without crowds. How easy to visit: MTR to nearby Lok Fu or Wong Tai Sin, short walk; free and accessible daily, actually I recommend taking a taxi there which is much quicker, I actually went there as a kid numerous times in the early 1970’s I was an explorer! I do not like the park but the Kowloon Walled City exhibition is well worth it

**Sham Shui Po District and Heritage of Mei Ho House** Vibrant working-class neighborhood full of authentic local life—electronics markets, fabric shops, street food, and old tenements - paired with the Heritage of Mei Ho House, a preserved 1954 public housing block turned youth hostel with a free museum exhibition on early resettlement life. It's a real hidden gem for understanding post-war Hong Kong social history without the crowds. Relevant: The museum recreates old apartment scenes with photos, documents, and videos of daily life in the 1950s-60s; combine with wandering Apliu Street or local eateries for an immersive half-day. How easy to visit: Extremely easy—MTR to Sham Shui Po Station (Exit B2) or Shek Kip Mei Station; Mei Ho House is an 8-10 minute walk. Museum generally open Tue-Sun 10am-6pm (free, self-guided); district is flat and compact and a tip for people that like climbing hundreds of steps, next to the exhibition building is a trail leading up to the hill behind the buildings, very scenic views over Kowloon, but those steps are nasty!

**The Peninsula Hotel** Hong Kong’s oldest hotel (opened 1928), affectionately known as the “Grande Dame of the Far East,” with the finest and most elegant lobbies in the city - many people talk about it but surprisingly few independent tourists actually step inside to experience it. Relevant: The soaring, light-filled lobby with tall columns, palm trees, string quartet music, and classic colonial grandeur offers a step back in time; it’s a living piece of Hong Kong history (including its role in WWII events) and a favorite for quiet appreciation or a memorable afternoon tea if desired. How easy to visit: Extremely easy - MTR to Tsim Sha Tsui Station (Exit H), then a 5 minute walk down Nathan Road; the lobby is freely accessible to visitors (dress respectfully, as it remains an operating luxury hotel); quick 15-30 minute stop that feels special without any planning hassle and outside the front doors, a fleet of Rolls Royces and Bentleys to convey guests.. it is one of my favourite places in Hong Kong and I have been there many thousands of times, the first time was January 2nd in 1972! and yes we have stayed there a couple of times!

Hong Kong rewards those who slow down just enough to notice the layers most visitors rush past. These 20 underrated spots aren’t about ticking off another checklist - they’re about giving you meaningful moments of calm views, living history, quiet heritage, and authentic local atmosphere without eating into your precious time or clashing with food tours and meals. Pick one or two that genuinely appeal to you, weave them around your existing plans, and you’ll likely come away with memories that feel far more personal than the usual crowded highlights.

If you only have 1 - 2 days, start with the easy Central and Peak clusters. If you have more time, mix in an island or a Kowloon neighborhood wander. Whatever you choose, I hope this list helps you experience a slightly different, more rewarding side of Hong Kong in 2026 - one that actually fits real travel days rather than sounding perfect on paper. enjoy every bite and every view along the way

So where are all the really weird places and hidden gems | secret spots in Hong Kong?

I did a lot of research and these places kept popping up as hidden gems and secret spots in Hong Kong but naturally no one goes into much detail about just how hard it is to get to them logistically and time wise.

**Sha Tau Kok a village on the physical border between Hong Kong and China**

**Lai Chi Wo Village old style place in the New Territories**

**Tsz Shan Monastery close to Tai Po but out in the middle of knowhere**

**Lam Tsuen Wishing Trees a very long way from town, but quite famous at Chinese New Year**.

These 4 places are in very remote areas and transport is a very real issue, some require booking in advance and Shau Tau Kok, well you need a permit from the Polic in Hong Kong 3 days in advance of your visit! - The Tsz Shan Monastery is wonderful but you have to book in advance and there is no flexibility to change as there are strict daily quota’s and taking public transport only gets you so far, there is a 30 minute walk uphill when you get off the bus

**Aberdeen Harbour (floating village area) - a shadow of it’s former self**

**PMQ (Former Police Married Quarters) - I simply do not understand why people find it interesting**

I could not believe that these 2 places got a lot of mentions, makes no sense at all, Aberdeen Harbour had a floating village of sorts over 50 yeats ago, not now and it is for me the worst kind of tourist trap and please note that the Jumbo Floating Restaurant which was iconic (and the only place worth visiting in Aberdeen) sank in 2022 near Cambodia! and PMQ is always jammed with tourists on free or low cost coach tours and it is simply not that interesting and is more of a craft store mall.

Finally, I also left this out - **Waterfall Bay Park (A Sky Full of Gods and Buddhas)** which was made famous when it was listed on the Atlas Obscura site, however it is in the heart of the Wah Fu Public Housing Estate which will be demolished shortly and many residents have moved out and Waterfall Bay was closed off with wire fences and barriers last time I went there, so I would not waste your time

Adding Night Markets & Evening Vibes to Your Day

After a full day exploring the underrated spots on this list - especially those on the Kowloon side or near Central - many visitors still have energy left for a relaxed evening wander. Hong Kong’s night markets offer a lively, atmospheric way to wind down, with plenty of street food, bargaining, and local flavor that pairs well with daytime sightseeing without requiring another big travel commitment.

The two most practical options for English-speaking visitors are:

**Temple Street Night Market** (Yau Ma Tei / Jordan area): This is the classic choice that still feels authentically local. Stalls typically start setting up from mid-afternoon and hit their stride around 7 - 8 pm, staying busy until 11 pm or midnight. You’ll find clothes, watches, gadgets, souvenirs, fortune tellers, and open-air dai pai dong-style dining with fresh seafood, claypot rice, and simple Cantonese dishes. It’s a great way to end a Kowloon-focused day (after Sham Shui Po, Wong Tai Sin Temple, or Kowloon Walled City Park) because it’s only a short MTR ride from many of those spots. The energy builds gradually, so you can arrive after dinner elsewhere or graze on-site and one of the Hong Kogn Governments great success stories was to introduce the street food section a few years ago, it is simply terrific

**Ladies’ Market** (Tung Choi Street, Mong Kok): Better suited for shopping than deep cultural immersion, with endless stalls selling clothes, bags, accessories, and casual souvenirs. It operates from afternoon into late evening (often until midnight). It’s easy to reach after a Kowloon day and works well if you want lighter browsing rather than full market chaos.

Yes, I do absolutely recommend them and I have always been a fan of Temple Street and all 3 sections of the market, oh and it is also a place where you can see if you look hard, lots of ladies plying their trade if you get my drift, I have been going there for over 50 years! (and stop snickering, to the market!)

These markets are compact enough that you can spend 45 - 90 minutes wandering without it feeling like a second full activity. They’re especially convenient if your daytime plans already have you on the Kowloon side - simply finish your last spot by late afternoon, grab an early dinner nearby if needed, then ease into the night market atmosphere. Bargaining is expected (start low and keep it friendly), and the casual street food scene means you can keep things light after a day of proper meals or a food tour.

A perfect low-key complement is a gentle evening stroll along the **Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade**. Just a short walk from The Peninsula Hotel or after any Kowloon daytime visit, the waterfront offers one of the best free night views of the Hong Kong Island skyline. The lights on the buildings create a glittering backdrop across the harbour, and the wide, paved path makes for an easy, enjoyable 20 - 40 minute walk. It’s rarely overwhelming in the evenings after 8pm, feels open and breezy, and gives a beautiful, relaxed close to the day - especially nice if you want something calmer than the markets and on the Avenue of the Stars is the iconic Bruce Lee Statue! Many people combine it with a quick stop at the Peninsula lobby earlier or use it as a peaceful wind-down before heading back to their hotel., and it is better anytime after 9pm

These evening options keep things flexible: do a daytime spot or two, enjoy your food plans, then add a market or promenade stroll only if the energy is there. No pressure, no long extra journeys - just an optional extension that lets you experience Hong Kong after dark in a manageable way

Final Thoughts from Jamie a Hong Kong Private Tour Guide and resident of Hong Kong for 50 + years and counting

With around **10 - 12 million foreign visitors expected in Hong Kong each year**, only a tiny fraction - perhaps 6,300 tours or so - are actually taken with private guides like myself and my 15 great friends in the business. That means the overwhelming majority of people choose to explore Hong Kong completely on their own, often relying on AI tools, travel blogs, or quick online research, it is hard to miss these tourists, phone held up to their face reading google maps or huddling together to check stuff on their smart phones.

I completely understand why. The internet has made it easier than ever to get around Hong Kong using public transport, discover places, and build your own itinerary. This list is my way of helping those independent travellers have a more realistic and rewarding experience - not by giving away “secrets,” but by sharing practical, time-smart suggestions that go beyond the usual crowded highlights and just a tad more useful information than offered by influencers, travel blogers and travel writers

At the end of the day, seeing Hong Kong purely on your own will always be a bit superficial. You’ll pick up some facts and nice photos, but you’ll miss the rich layers of history, the personal stories behind the buildings, the local insights, and the “why” behind so many things that make this city unique. That’s where a great private tour makes all the difference - turning a pleasant day into an awesome, memorable one and with stories to tell when you get back home.

If this post has helped you plan smarter and enjoy Hong Kong a little more deeply, then I’m glad. And if you decide you’d like to go beyond the surface and really understand the stories that bring these places to life, booking a private tour with an experienced local private tour guide remains one of the best investments you can make in your trip.

visit Hong Kong and enjoy the food, the views, and the unexpected quiet corners - and we hope to cross paths with some of you out there on the streets of Hong Kong.

Jamie’s i hHong Kong - Pearl of the Orient and my home for over 50+ years

# **I do not do Food Tours in Hong Kong but I know people that do!**

I do not do food tours as mentioned above, I have very specific reasons and part of it is that I do not speak Cantonese or write Chinese, I am from Yorkshire in England and I lack the language gene and it is not through lack of trying and yes a lot of restaurants do not have English menu’s or staff who speak conversational English.

.. and yet I have eaten at close to 1,400 restaurants in Hong Kong since January 2nd 1972, my wife was born in Hong Kong and we have been together over 40 years and her first language is Cantonese and a lot of her family are Chinese or half Chinese so I have never had much of an issue!

This does not translate to doing food tours though, yes, I could do them, no problem there but they would never ever be as good as the food tours done by my friends (see the 3 links above) most of their awesome guides are locally born Hong Kong Chinese and obviously food culture is part of their DNA, it is impossible for me to compete with that!

So please feel free to contact them for food tours

**I do not do Hiking Tours in Hong Kong but I know someone that does!**

I do not do Hiking Tours, never have and never will even though I used to go Hiking a lot when I was a lot younger, The Hong Kong Government is promoting hiking tours so I urge you to contact my friend Sabrina at Hong Kong Trails and Tours, she is a long time Hong Kong resident and and a very experienced hiker with close to **700 Hikes in 15+ years** under her belt, please click on the link below

results.title: THE 15 BEST Hong Kong Food Tours (with Prices) - Tripadvisor
results.content:
Thank you Sandy for a wonderful tour.  Read more     Review of: Hong Kong's Tin Hau Hidden Gems Food Tour with 7+ Local Tastings Written March 29, 2026      
   Image 82      Paradise302376568172 contributions    0        
### Thoughtfully Designed and Fun Tour

The food tour was great, particularly our guide, Truly. She did an excellent job of planning the tour and highlighting the key parts of the city and cultural significance. This wasn’t just a “let’s go to 4 or 5 restaurants” tour, but a real tour, going to places unique to the city. Well planned and well executed.  Read more     Review of: Best Street Food Tour With a Local Guide in Hong Kong Written May 11, 2026      

See All Tour Operators

When to visit [...] However, our guide was not the greatest and the group was not really engaged. The guide didn’t allow us to really talk and have an actual conversation with each other or about the food. It felt like we were bombarded with Wikipedia answers with no room to breath and experience the moment. 

If you are at all familiar with Asian culture and food, this tour isn’t for you.  Read more     Review of: Hong Kong's Tin Hau Hidden Gems Food Tour with 7+ Local Tastings Written July 12, 2026      
   Image 71      Christian H4 contributions    0        
### Great tour [...] My friend and I booked this last min and had a GREAT time with Eric our guide. The food was amazing and all the culture and history we learned along the way. I highly recommend this tour, and its a wonderful intro into the city, understanding the key food items of Hong Kong tasting savory to sweet! It's an excellent starting point if you've never been here. Thank you so much HK Food Tour and Eric! This is a must!!!  Read more     Review of: Hong Kong Food Tour: Central and Sheung Wan Districts Written March 25, 2026      
   Image 75      magik930Los Angeles, CA 4 contributions    0        
### A great tour with great food!
