### [Book it. Now. Zero regrets and a lasting experience.](https://www.tripadvisor.com/ShowUserReviews-g294217-d11470913-r1056963010-Hong_Kong_Food_Tour_Sham_Shui_Po_District-Hong_Kong.html)

With a weekend in HK, the pressure was on to cram as much local culture and cuisine in as possible. In a world of AI and with an analytical personality, I spent the Friday evening overwhelmed by the multitude of choices. In a fit of panic at midnight, I desperately searched for a foodie tour that would deliver on my (admittedly) impossible expectations. I found a Reddit thread that recommended HK foodie tasting tours and took a chance. What serendipity! From the warm welcome when I was almost late (they recommend you arrive 15m before start time) Silvana’s deep expertise, patience and perfect balance between enough local treats and morsels of cultural insights - struck me as exceptional. Helpfully, the group also provides a brochure that describes all the stops and some phrases in Cantonese. We tried a number of local dishes in Sham Shui po, a working class neighbourhood humming with people going about their daily business. At every eatery we ate, the owners and hosts were welcoming and the food really granted my wish of experiencing the full range of HK Cuisine. I am a vegetarian and Silvana expertly and diligently filtered out any dishes with lard. Sometimes, food tours leave you overstuffed. While I will definitely be skipping lunch, this was the exact right amount of food. Silvana struck the perfect rhythm of delicious food AND allowing you to absorb the area. She shared its history (sprinkled with personal perspective) and fascinating titbits to help explore the textured layers of a community. At the end of the tour she posed what she framed as a difficult question- to choose a favourite. After I ranked my choices- she exclaimed: that’s all of them! And I think that summed up the craft of a foodie tour exquisitely delivered. I am so grateful for an experience which gave me a glimpse into a new and rich world and strongly recommend this tour. I particularly enjoyed the dim sum tea house (where they bring round the dim sum on trolleys and you have to almost grab what you want as they go past) it felt so authentic and was really great to see. We would never have found it or gone in on our own so it was fantastic to visit. I’ve had dim sum in the past a lot, but this was so much more tasty than any I’d had before.

Alex was very nice and gave us lots of information. He even took photos of the group and sent us a little combination video afterwards which was a lovely thing to do and went above and beyond his duties as a guide.

I would highly recommend this tour with Alex as I really enjoyed all of the food we had (and the small local family run venues we tried them in - which we probably would have hesitated about going in on our own) and also it was great as we also experienced going on one of their skinny trams together (which I hadn’t been quite sure how to use) and we also went up one of the iconic escalators. So we saw quite a bit of Hong Kong we might have missed otherwise.

We both said it was one of our holiday highlights. ### [Tasty Tin Hau](https://www.tripadvisor.com/ShowUserReviews-g294217-d13203915-r1054698986-Hong_Kong_s_Tin_Hau_Hidden_Gems_Food_Tour_with_7_Local_Tastings-Hong_Kong.html)

We’ve had an informative and tasty tour with Sandy in Tin Hau. Our small group of 9 people was welcomed by Sandy warm and in time. After a short time queueing at Sister Wah’s noodle kitchen we’ve had the maybe best brisket noodles soup ever in that Michelin recommended restaurant. After strolling through Tin Hau we’ve also tried roasted goose. Really tasty. An amazing experience was a short visit at the wet market. Such a great selection of fresh ingredients, like poultry, pork, beef, fish and shellfish. Very impressive was the experience of having tea and dumplings at a local tea house. Inclusive a beauty treatment by having some chicken feet for an extra person of collagen. 

Finally we have learned from Sandy some Cantonese and a few helpful hints about Chinese cuisine and culture. 

Thank you Sandy for a wonderful tour. results.title: 15 Incredible Things to Do in Hong Kong: The 2026 Edition
results.content:
The building itself has 118 stories and is found in the heart of Hong Kong. Its central location makes it the perfect spot to lay your eyes on the entire city as you stand 1,300 feet (393m) above sea level. Make the journey during the day to see the lush green mountains and the busy harbor. Or visit at night to look down upon a city lit up like a star-filled sky.

## Eat All Night at Chungking Mansions

The entrance to the mansions may leave a lot to be desired, but that will soon be forgotten as you surround yourself with endless aisles of food. The extensive building complex is home to immigrants from all over the world, a place that has shed its dodgy reputation to become a one stop shop for authentic local and international cuisine. [...] ## Take a Trip to Tai O

On neighboring Lantau Island, the small fishing village of Tai O presents life as it was long before the Opium War. On the island’s west end, you couldn’t be further removed from the modern day hustle and bustle of Hong Kong. The quiet streets showcase a traditional way of life, the perfect complement to your tour of colonial architecture.

In Tai O, many of the local homes are built of stilts that rise out of the waterways. In front of each home is a small dinghy, the primary form of transport from home to local markets. Although popular with tourists, it remains a poignant and peaceful experience. [...] Grab some snacks and maybe even some hot cocoa, sit back and enjoy the show. There are over 40 buildings that take part in the nightly event. The symphony aims to take you on a unique journey that showcases the spirit, diversity and energy of Hong Kong. All up, there are five scenes, with the final act an incredible synchronized display of kaleidoscopic patterns.

## Shop and Eat in Tsim Sha Tsui

Before or after the captivating Symphony of Lights, take the time to explore Tsim Sha Tsui, an exciting shopping and entertainment district. Running along the harbor front, Tsim Sha Tsui is the heart of the youthful Kowloon.
results.raw_content:
![Never Ending Footsteps](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_3000,h_600/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Copy-of-Copy-of-Untitled-1.png)
![Never Ending Footsteps](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%203000%20600%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

# 15 Incredible Things to Do in Hong Kong: The 2026 Edition

Hong Kong is one of the most thrilling places I’ve ever visited.

I mean, let’s face it: this is a destination that full-on *slaps* you in the senses from the moment you arrive. There’s the noise, the crowds, the incense, the neon, plus the sheer density of the skyscrapers. It’s intense, overwhelming and, if you’re anything like me, leaves you feeling more alive than ever before.

Hong Kong never fails to remind me why I love travel so much. I can’t get enough of that feeling of discombobulation, wandering through unfamiliar streets, half-lost, absolutely overstimulated, and just trying to process everything that’s going on around me.

And yet, despite the mayhem that Hong Kong is so famous for, it’s surprisingly easy to escape from it all and take a deep breath. Clamber up a hiking trail, wander into a quiet temple, take a seat within a lush park; all of a sudden, you’ll feel the city exhale. *Peace*.

That contrast is one of my favourite things about Hong Kong: the hyper-dense city life paired with an unexpected amount of calming green spaces.

It’s a city that rewards repeat visitors, too. The more time I spend in Hong Kong, the more I realise it’s impossible to see it all. There are hidden art galleries tucked above shops, tiny rooftop cafes with skyline views you can’t find in a guidebook, secret hiking paths that open onto quiet waterfalls, and night markets that feel completely different depending on which neighbourhood they’re in.

It feels like every single time I visit this city, I come away with a list of 30 *more* things that have managed to blow my mind. After my most recent trip, then, I knew I wanted to finally put this guide together: an in-depth list of all of my favourite things to do in Hong Kong, from the chaotic to the calm, and everything in between.

Let’s get stuck in.

## Ride the Star Ferry

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_1050/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Hong-Kong-Star-Ferry.jpg)

The immense Hong Kong skyline is a breathtaking sight. But rather than gawk up at the towering skyscrapers from land, capture it on a bright green ferry. The Star Ferry has been operating along the beautiful Victoria Harbour since the late 19th century and remains one of the best things to do in Hong Kong.

The inexpensive experience and takes you out into the harbor, one bustling with maritime activity. The word busy doesn’t quite capture the atmosphere, as boats, ships and tankers share the water. But life on Victoria Harbour is nonetheless refreshing as the cool breeze laps through your hair, a lovely cure to time spent in the dense city core.

Star Ferry’s main route runs from Kowloon to Hong Kong. As you peer towards the island, prepare to be struck by the sheer size of the lush mountains soaring behind the elegant towers. Take the ferry towards the city at night to see the mesmerizing Symphony of Lights.

## Summit Victoria Peak

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_923/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/The-Peak-Hong-Kong.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%20923%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

Known simply as the Peak by locals, the mountain soars to 1600 feet (490m) above Hong Kong, providing equally breathtaking vistas as her namesake’s harbor. The famous city is predominantly flat, and from the viewpoint you’ll have it laid out in front of you in spectacular fashion.

But first, you must reach the summit. There are several ways to complete this task, some more arduous than others. Depending on the time you have (and your willingness to walk), you may want to complete the trek up the mountain. The path is paved and cuts through the Central district for 1.6 miles (2.6km) to the edge of Victoria Peak.

It will take around an hour to complete. But as you’ll soon see, Hong Kong’s stifling heat may force a change of plans. There are multiple buses that will take you to the summit, but for the most scenic views, take the funicular Peak Tram, that’s been running since 1888.

## Visit Wong Tai Sin Temple

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_947/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/Wong-Tai-Sin-Temple-entrance-Hong-Kong.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%20947%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

In Kowloon, the Wong Sai Tin Temple was one of the more recent additions to Hong Kong’s list of spiritual sites. The first iteration of this temple was developed at the beginning of the 20th century, however, it was entirely replaced by a new temple in 1968.

The temple celebrates Wong Tai Sin, a revered Taoist god who is thought to bring good luck and help heal the sick. Visitors can explore the colorful grounds, home to many sites such as the beautiful Good Wish Garden and the Hall of Three Saints. The centerpiece, however, is an expansive hall that honors Confucius along with his dozens of disciples. As you wander inside, you will pass several fortune-tellers alongside rows of joss sticks and incense candles emitting a powerful aroma throughout the hall.

In September, the temple hosts the Mid-Autumn Festival in honor of Wong Tai Sin’s birthday. A celebration that involves lighting large swaths of lanterns and releasing them from the temple grounds.

## Experience Colonial Hong Kong

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_1050/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Victoria-Prison-Hong-Kong.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%201050%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

In the Opium War of the 1830s, Britain invaded China. One of its first major moves was to occupy Hong Kong. A few years later, in 1841, the city state officially became a British colony. It would stay that way for the better part of 150 years.

Hong Kong has become a modern metropolis in every sense of the world. The city is at the forefront of international business and its teeming with 21st century architecture. Perhaps then, the most intriguing part of the city is the old pastel-hued colonial buildings.

Before the British, Hong Kong was a collection of small fishing villages. However, the newcomers had other ideas. Under colonial rule, the island was developed at a rapid pace and many of the historic buildings remain today. On Hong Kong Island, you’ll see numerous examples, including multiple churches, the embellished facades at HSBC, along with the government buildings. All of which lay in direct contrast to the rest of the city.

Take in the best sites while learning all about Hong Kong’s colonial past on this [walking tour](https://www.viator.com/tours/Hong-Kong-SAR/Sheung-Wan-and-Central-Walking-Tour-Into-the-Colonial-Hong-Kong/d35959-147755P1?localeSwitch=1&pid=P00168038&mcid=42383&medium=link&campaign=hong-kong-activities).

## Take a Trip to Tai O

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1500,h_1000/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/Tai-O-fishing-village-Hong-Kong-edited.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201500%201000%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

On neighboring Lantau Island, the small fishing village of Tai O presents life as it was long before the Opium War. On the island’s west end, you couldn’t be further removed from the modern day hustle and bustle of Hong Kong. The quiet streets showcase a traditional way of life, the perfect complement to your tour of colonial architecture.

In Tai O, many of the local homes are built of stilts that rise out of the waterways. In front of each home is a small dinghy, the primary form of transport from home to local markets. Although popular with tourists, it remains a poignant and peaceful experience.

Here, it’s common for locals to offer rides along the tidal flats and see the village from their perspective. Afterwards, head to the local market, where you’ll be able to try some fresh catch long before it makes its way to the big city.

Aside from experiencing the fishing side of life, slightly inland you’ll discover a lovely community and some of the best street food going around. Some of the must try treats included salted fish and the Tai O Husband. The latter is a pizza roll of shrimp and pork.

## Climb Up to Big Buddha

![Tian Tan Buddha in Hong Kong](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1024,h_680/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/01/IMG_6782_3_4_tonemapped-1-1024x680.jpg)
![Tian Tan Buddha in Hong Kong](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201024%20680%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

Just outside of Tai O, on Lantau Island, is one of the biggest statues of Buddha on earth. The statue was completed in 1993 after 12 painstaking years of construction. High above the Po Lin Temple, Big Buddha stands at 111 feet tall (34m). In what was once a remote part of the island, the huge addition has completely changed the tourist’s path.

You can spot the Tian Tan Buddha Statue from an astounding distance. The only source of scale is the nearby forest, which is completely dwarfed by the monument. But when standing beneath Big Buddha, that’s when you truly understand the magnitude of it and how Tian Tan is an immense feat of engineering.

The best way to arrive at the landmark is via the Ngong Ping cable car. A scenic 25-minute ride takes you high above the forest and across the mountains before dropping you off at Ngong Ping village. The small town focuses heavily on tourism, somewhat diminishing the experience, however it will quickly be forgotten as you walk up an enormous staircase to the bottom of Big Buddha.

## Have a Beach Day

Hong Kong can be such a hectic place to be that you’ll sometimes forget to take time to relax. For some rest and recreation, why not spend a day at the beach? Beach days and Hong Kong might not sound like two things that go together, but it’s actually one of the best ways to experience the city like a local.

The top choice among residents is Repulse Bay, a place that isn’t the slightest bit repulsive. On its golden sand, you can enjoy splendid views and calm swimming among nobody but locals, as few travelers are willing to say goodbye to the captivating action of downtown.

While the city is full of opulence and big money, there is an air of luxury to the streets around Repulse Beach. Beyond the sand, you’ll find an extravagant beachfront boulevard home to up-scale restaurants and trendy boutiques. Kick back and enjoy some delicious eats on a spacious patio that overlooks the bay.

## Complete Dragon’s Back Hike

Another popular beach to visit is Big Wave Bay. Here, beachgoers have the opportunity to trek along a section of the Dragon’s Back, one of the top hikes in Hong Kong. But if you’re someone who likes to strap on the hiking boots and complete a trail, then why not do the whole thing?

Dragon’s Back is a 5.6 miles (3.5km) moderate trail that conveniently begins at the Tei Wan bus top. Dragon’s Back trail guides you through Shek O Country Park and is a 2.5 hour journey. Along the way, the trail showcases the best of Hong Kong’s nature. Thanks to a series of rolling mountains, forests and tropical beaches that will change how you see the city state.

The majority of the trek is downhill as you make your way towards Big Wave Bay. After capturing wondrous views of the beach, you’ll have the chance to recuperate on its sandy shores or make the short trip to Shek O, a town that’s known for its fresh seafood.

## Check Out the Symphony of Lights

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_1028/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/Aqua-Luna-Hong-Kong.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%201028%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

Every night, when the clock strikes 8, the Hong Kong skyline lights up for a spectacular show. Since beginning in 2004, the Symphony of Lights has gone onto become one of the top light shows on earth.

Unless you’ve time your trip on the Green Ferry perfectly, we recommend arriving early at the Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade. From your spot on Victoria Harbour, you’ll have a great vantage point, with panoramic views of Hong Kong Island and the soaring Victoria Peak.

Grab some snacks and maybe even some hot cocoa, sit back and enjoy the show. There are over 40 buildings that take part in the nightly event. The symphony aims to take you on a unique journey that showcases the spirit, diversity and energy of Hong Kong. All up, there are five scenes, with the final act an incredible synchronized display of kaleidoscopic patterns.

## Shop and Eat in Tsim Sha Tsui

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_933/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/Bruce-Lee-statue-Hong-Kong-edited.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%20933%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

Before or after the captivating Symphony of Lights, take the time to explore Tsim Sha Tsui, an exciting shopping and entertainment district. Running along the harbor front, Tsim Sha Tsui is the heart of the youthful Kowloon.

As the newer side of Hong Kong, Kowloon combines a wide range of international culture and modern amenities. It showcases how the city is often seen on the world stage, a cosmopolitan city and a thriving commercial hub.

The district’s main street is Nathan Road. Here, you’ll find rows of trendy restaurants serving anything from hearty kebabs to traditional eats. Between the restaurants are independent fashion outlets and several malls. For high-end shopping, make your way down Canton Road.

Other major attractions in the area include the iconic Clock Tower and the Hong Kong Space Museum. Aside from it being a hub of food and great shopping, Tsim Sha Tsui is one of the best places to base yourself throughout your Hong Kong experience.

## Relax in Hong Kong Park

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_1050/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Vantage-Point-in-Hong-Kong-Park.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%201050%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

Just blocks away from the revelry on Lan Kwai Fong, Hong Kong Park is where you should go to rest the head and enjoy some fresh air. In a city that never slows down, it’s nice to know that such a tranquil place exists. Spread along the foothills of Victoria Peak, you’ll find ample walking paths leading you alongside ponds and through forests to peaceful gardens.

Visitors will find room to hang with their traveling crew, while solo adventurers can grab some shade, read a book or watch the world go by. If the hangover is subsiding, then you’ll be up to experiencing one of the highlights of Hong Kong Park, its aviary. An outdoor area that’s shrouded in clear netting, the aviary is home to over 80 bird species. The elevated path takes you high into the canopy, where you’ll stand alongside the birds that are chirping away on the branches.

## Explore the Chi Lin Nunnery

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_1050/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/Chi-Lin-Nunnery-Hong-Kong-2.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%201050%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

Another way to seek some peace and quiet in Hong Kong is to visit the Chi Lin Nunnery. Also one of the city’s most unique attractions, the Buddhist monastery was originally built in the 1930s. However, to honor the style and designs of the Tang Dynasty, Chi Lin was rebuilt from scratch using nothing but wood and not a single nail.

The Chi Lin Nunnery that you see today first opened in 1998. The complex draws you in from the moment you enter Sam Mun. Its entrance is a series of three gates that represent “skillful means”, wisdom and compassion. Continue on until you gaze upon gorgeous lotus ponds, bougainvillea and bonsai tea plants.

Before long, you’ll reach the Hall of Celestial Kings, where you’ll find a statue of Buddha and several deities. The silent nuns preserve the tranquility as they deliver offerings to Buddha before chanting out of sight.

## Take in the Views at Sky100

Hong Kong is home to several amazing viewpoints, including the harbor and the peak. But to complete the experience, make your way to the tallest building in the city. Sky100 is an observation deck that offers 360-degree views of the skyline, Kowloon, the harbour and surrounding mountains.

The building itself has 118 stories and is found in the heart of Hong Kong. Its central location makes it the perfect spot to lay your eyes on the entire city as you stand 1,300 feet (393m) above sea level. Make the journey during the day to see the lush green mountains and the busy harbor. Or visit at night to look down upon a city lit up like a star-filled sky.

## Eat All Night at Chungking Mansions

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_1032/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/Chungking-Mansions-entrance.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%201032%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

The entrance to the mansions may leave a lot to be desired, but that will soon be forgotten as you surround yourself with endless aisles of food. The extensive building complex is home to immigrants from all over the world, a place that has shed its dodgy reputation to become a one stop shop for authentic local and international cuisine.

In Kowloon, Chungking Mansions is teeming with dozens of food stalls set up by newcomers to the city. Using recipes from the four corners of the globe, you can literally eat your way around the world.

Add in a variety of market stalls to shop for random trinkets and a hostel full of backpackers, it’s easy to see how your night at Chungking Mansions will go.

## Embark on a day trip to Macau

![](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1400,h_1050/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/grand-lisboa-hotel-macau.jpg)
![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201400%201050%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

It’s hard to imagine that a city like Macau is so close to Hong Kong. A city with a rich past, Portuguese colonialism and now a gambling mecca. It’s easy to arrive on the shores of Macau, with several ferries departing Hong Kong each day. Better yet, why not cross the longest sea-crossing bridge in the world?

You won’t find a shortage of things to do in the city state. It’s enchanting old architecture could keep you busy for days on end, as you venture to the ruins of St. Paul’s Cathedral and see the amazing facades surrounding Senado Square.

But in recent years, Macau has become known as the “Las Vegas of the East”, and for good reason. Here you’ll find endless entertainment, from the dancing fountains at Wynn Palace (just like the Bellagio) to the world’s highest bungee jump at the Macau Tower. Macau is also the gambling capital of the world, with over 70% of its GDP coming from casinos. So are you feeling lucky?

## Meet Lauren Juliff

![Lauren Juliff at Australia's 12 Apostles at sunset](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1024,h_768/https://www.neverendingfootsteps.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/Lauren-Juliff-12-Apostles-sunset-copy-2-1024x768.jpg)
![Lauren Juliff at Australia's 12 Apostles at sunset](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%201024%20768%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

I travel independently to countries most people skip, and write about what those places are actually like on the ground.

Since quitting my job in 2011, I've visited more than 100 countries and built this site around first-hand reporting: real costs, real experiences, and honest accounts of what's worth doing and what's absolutely not.

I pay for all my travels myself, don't take sponsored trips, and travel anonymously; all to ensure my recommendations are ones you can trust.

See the gilded wooden structures and meticulous Tang architecture stand out against the backdrop of massive residential skyscrapers offers a unique visual perspective on Hong Kong.

Atmosphere: A real serenity emerges from the place. Despite its central geographical location, the garden manages to isolate the visitor from the surrounding urban tumult.

Aesthetics: The maintenance of bonsai trees, rocks and bodies of water is impeccable, respecting the principles of classical Chinese gardening.

It is an indispensable step for anyone looking for a moment of calm and cultural immersion without leaving the metropolis. Built in 1934 for the retreat of Buddhist nuns and rebuilt in 1998, the place is splendid. 

It is one of the world’s largest hand-crafted wooden buildings, with no need for nails. Covering an area of more than 30,000 m2, there are about fifteen buildings including a library, a tower, a bell tower, a school, among others. 

The inner courtyards, symmetrical, with vases, pools with water lilies are splendid. Statues of gold, clay and wood can be found in the various buildings. 

You can gather in many halls and, beyond the spiritual side of the convent, it must be recognized that the place is very photogenic, with the mountain in the background in particular. the best prices. Number two, get some cash. ATMs usually offer better exchange rates than airport 
money changers, so we prefer withdrawing upon arrival, but don't withdraw too much because Hong 
Kong is pretty credit card friendly. You'll only really need cash for street food stalls, night 
markets, and tiny local shops. And number three, don't forget to pick up an octopus card. This is 
your golden ticket to getting around Hong Kong on public transportation. Yes, you can also tap your 
credit card on some train stations, but it doesn't work on most buses and ferries around the city, 
so it's less reliable. That's why it's better to just grab an Octopus card and tap everywhere like 
a local. You can use it on basically everything. the MTR, which is Hong Kong Subway, buses, 
ferries, trams, even convenience stores, cafes, and some restaurants across the city. You can 
easily buy a standard Octopus card at the airport, any MTR station, or even convenience 
stores. It costs around 20 USD per card, which is $150 Hong Kong dollars. And it already 
includes $100 Hong Kong dollars stored value, plus 50 Hong Kong dollars refundable deposit. Just 
top it up, tap to ride, and you're good to go. And if you've got any leftover balance at the end 
of your trip, you can refund both your remaining balance and your deposit at any MTR service center 
or at the airport arrivals hall counter. There's also a tourist octopus card which works exactly 
the same, but it's non-refundable and cost around 5 USD. So, some people just keep it as a souvenir. 
Most travelers still prefer the standard Octopus card since you could refund your money back. 
Whichever card you use, the Google Maps app will be your best friend for navigating around

Well, there are three main ways to getting into the city. Option one, you can take the train. 
The Hong Kong Airport Express is fast, smooth, and gets you straight to Hong Kong station in

depending on your drop off point. Here are the most popular airport bus routes along with their 
prices and schedules. Feel free to screenshot. And the best part, you can easily tap your 
Octopus card on the bus and hop right in. Now, option three. If you've got heavy luggage or 
your flight is arriving super late, booking a taxi is a good option. They're widely available 
and metered. Plus, they're super cute. Just expect to pay around $35 to $45 one way. However, be 
aware not all drivers speak English fluently. So, it can be helpful to have your destination written 
in Cantonese or use a translator app. Otherwise, you can book rides directly using the Uber app 
and simply pin your exact destination to avoid any miscommunication. Now, if you want true zero 
stress, I highly recommend pre-booking a private airport transfer. You've got a driver waiting for 
you right at arrivals. It's surprisingly about the same price as a taxi and an Uber, and the cars 
are spacious enough for four to six people. This is perfect for families, oversized luggage, or

chat with your driver. Okay, so now you're an expert on how to get to the city. Now, it's time

there are four main areas I would recommend. So, let me show you the differences between each one 
so it can help you choose the best area for you. Let's begin. First up, Tsim Sha Tsui. This is the 
best all-around area for first- timers. It's Hong Kong in full color. Harbour views, giant malls,

So, if you want convenience and that classic Victoria Harbour skyline, TST is the safest 
bet. Next up, Central. This is best for modern Hong Kong vibes. It's sleek, stylish, and a bit

linked to the Airport Express, making arrivals and departures effortless. And you'll feel Hong 
Kong's New York energy with its glossy skyscrapers and fast-paced crowds. Perfect for travelers 
who want a modern upscale scene. Next up, Mong Kok. This is best for foodies, night markets, 
and budget stays. If you want full-on chaotic, beautiful Hong Kong energy, MongKok is it. This 
is home to lively night markets, sneakers street, cheap eats on every corner, and neon lit 
everything. It's more local, more crowded, and more colorful with tons of budget lodging 
around. So, if you're on a tight budget and love exploring night markets and street food, 
Mongkok will be your playground. And finally, Causeway Bay. This is best for shoppers and 
youthful energy. Think of it as Hong Kong's shopping paradise. Malls everywhere, bright 
billboards, busy crosswalks, and non-stop buzz. It's young, trendy, and a bit chaotic like Hong 
Kong's version of Seoul's Myeongdong with cool cafes on every corner and endless shops to 
explore. It's perfect if you want a lively, modern area packed with things to do. Of course, 
Hong Kong has plenty of other great neighborhoods, too, like Wan Chai for a more local everyday 
city vibe, or Lantau if you prefer nature, beaches, and a quieter escape. But if it's your 
first time in the city, these four areas offer the best mix of everything Hong Kong is known 
for. The city is incredibly well-connected. So, as long as you're staying near an MTR station, all 
the main tour spots are just a quick train ride away. I'll drop some affordable hotel links near

Jordan, literally a 10-second walk away from the Jordan MTR and just one stop away from Mongkok. 
Our room was only $65 per night for two people. It was small, but pretty standard for budget hotels 
in Hong Kong. What mattered most was that it was clean, the location was amazing, and the reviews 
were pretty solid. They even offer a smart TV with Netflix, YouTube, and free drinking water refills 
in the lobby. Still our favorite budget hack. The AGODA app offers some of the cheapest rates, and 
you can use our coupon code WANDERSAGODA for an extra discount. Okay, so now that you've 
booked your flights, booked your hotels, and got your transport cards, let's move on to 
the next question. What are the best things to do in Hong Kong? And how much will I spend? Whether 
you're here for skyline views, epic street food, or theme parks, Hong Kong has something for 
everyone. So, let's dive in to the top 10 most popular things to do in Hong Kong, including their 
price tags. and stick around until number one because this is something you absolutely cannot 
miss. Let's start the countdown off at number 10. No Hong Kong trip is complete without a dose of 
Disney magic. Hong Kong Disneyland may be smaller than its global siblings, but that's exactly what 
makes it charming. No rushing through giant parks, no getting lost for half a day, just pure easy 
magical fun. Tickets usually go for around $80 to $100 depending on the season. And the rides are 
perfect for all ages, from adrenaline junkies to toddlers and everyone in between. You can also eat 
themed snacks, catch character meet and greets, shop for limited edition Hong Kong merch, and stay 
till the finale fireworks light up the sky. It's wholesome, heartwarming, and pure nostalgia. 
Whether you're 5 years old or a grown adult, it still hits hard when Mickey waves at you. 
Number nine, part aquarium, part theme park, and part giant zoo. Ocean Park Hong Kong 
is basically three adventures in one. From adorable pandas and playful sea lions to massive 
jellyfish and polar animals, it's like jumping between continents in a single day. Tickets cost 
$60 per head. And if you're visiting with a group, you can score discounts for three people or more 
using our discount link in the description. But here's what makes Ocean Park extra exciting. After 
riding that cliffside cable car with sweeping sea views, you're dropped off straight into a world 
of adrenaline with roller coasters that literally look like it's hanging off a cliff and a tower 
that launches you above the coastline for a view of the entire park. One minute you're saying hi 
to the cute penguins and the next minute you're clinging onto your seat and questioning your 
life's decisions. Number eight. If you're into jaw-dropping, literal head in the clouds moment, 
hop onto the Ngong Ping 360 cable car in Lantau Island. This 25-minute aerial journey glides over 
mountains, forests, and the sea, ending at the peaceful Ngong Ping village. A standard cabin cost 
about $23 one way, and the crystal cabin with a glass floor is $27. But guys, I highly recommend 
upgrading to the Crystal Plus cabin at $31. It has full 360° glass walls and a glass floor. So, 
yep, you'll be floating and seeing exactly how high you are. It's kind of scary, kind of funny, 
but honestly, we're so glad we booked this one. And here's a little budget hack. Book the 
standard cabin one way and the crystal plus cabin on the way back. You still get 
that crystal clear gondola experience, but you'll save money on half the ride. Once you 
reach the top, visit the Tian Tan Big Buddha, one of the largest outdoor seated Buddhas in the 
world, and climb up the steps for a truly majestic view. Then walk over to the Po Lin Monastery 
for some temple food, explore the village shops, and just soak in the slower mountain vibes. 
It's the perfect escape from the busy city. Number seven. If you're looking for the 
most iconic skyline view in Hong Kong, head straight to Victoria Peak, the city's highest 
point with crazy panoramic views of skyscrapers, Victoria Harbour, and lush mountains that make 
Hong Kong look like a futuristic jungle. The best way to get there is via the Peak Tram, a 
super steep funicular ride where the buildings look like they're tilted sideways because of how 
steep the track is. Roundtrip combo tickets for the Peak Tram plus the Sky Terrace 428 viewpoint 
go for around $19. And for an extra discount, you can use the coupon code AngelicaWandersKlook 
to save a little more. Once you reach the top, take in those 360° views. Snap those postcard 
worthy photos and stay until sunset to watch the skyline transform into a glittering light 
show. Number six, experience Hong Kong's best museums. Art lovers and history buffs, this one's 
for you. Start in West Kowloon at M+ Museum, a hub for contemporary visual culture. Think giant 
installations, digital art, Asian pop culture, and quirky photography. Tickets start at 
around 25 bucks, and exhibitions change often, so there's always something new to see. Then, 
right next door is the Hong Kong Palace Museum, where you can admire centuries old treasures from 
Beijing's forbidden city, gold antiques, intricate pottery, ancient paintings. It's like stepping 
into history without flying to mainland China. A standard entrance is around 9 bucks and some 
special exhibits may cost extra, but they're worth it if you're into royal treasures and dynasties. 
Number five, if there's one budget friendly must-do in Hong Kong, it's sailing across Victoria 
Harbour on the iconic Star Ferry. You can take a ride from TST to Central and it's super quick, 
just a 5 to 10 minute ride and costs less than a street snack. But the views unreal. You get front 
row seats to skyscrapers, mountain silhouettes, and Hong Kong's most famous landmarks on both 
sides of the harbour. For a full cinematic moment, go at sunset or catch the Symphony of Light Show, 
which runs every evening at 8:00 p.m. It only lasts about 10 minutes, but the entire harbor 
sparkles while you cruise through it. Cheap, scenic, iconic, and you can easily tap in and out 
with your octopus card. Hassle-free. Number four, walk the Avenue of Stars at Tsim Sha Tsui's 
waterfront. Picture the Hollywood Walk of Fame, but with a stunning waterfront backdrop. As you 
stroll along Tsim Sha Tsui's famous promenade, you'll get some of the best nighttime views in 
Hong Kong with neon reflections dancing on the water. Welcome to the Avenue of Stars, the 
city's tribute to its legendary film icons like Bruce Lee, Jackie Chan, Michelle Yeoh, and 
more. It's completely free, perfect for photos, a mix of sightseeing, and a relaxing golden hour 
walk, all in one stop. Number three, feast at a discounted hotel buffet. Hong Kong loves buffets, 
and we're not talking about sad salad bars. We're talking overflowing seafood mountains, sushi 
stations, roast meats, dim sum counters, and never ending desserts. And let me put you in on a little 
secret. Many luxury hotels offer discounted seats if you pre-book it online. And I'm talking 40 to 
50% off. You got the Marriott, Sheraton, Cordis, Harbour Grand, and many more. A lunch buffet 
at the Marriott normally costs $60 per person, but you can pre-book it online for only 30 bucks. 
We'll link down some of the best buffet deals in the description. And another budget tip, book 
weekday lunch slots. They're the cheapest, but they do fill up fast because Hong Kongers 
are serious about their buffets. Number two, one of our favorites, take a day trip to Macau. 
Just an hour by ferry or 30 minutes by bus, Macau is one of the easiest and coolest day trips 
you could take from Hong Kong. Known as the Las Vegas of Asia, it blends dazzling casino resorts 
with beautifully preserved Portuguese heritage. In a single afternoon, you can wander through gold 
lined luxury hotels with indoor gondolas. Then step into cobblestone streets surrounded by pastel 
buildings and centuries old UNESCO landmarks. Don't miss iconic spots like the ruins of St. 
Paul's, Senado Square, the colorful Taipa Village, and of course, you just have to try the famous 
egg tarts from Lord Stow's Bakery. Ferries and buses from Hong Kong cost around the same price, 
around 20 to $25 oneway. Whether you're visiting Macau for a day or staying overnight, the mix 
of glitzy city life, European architecture, and seriously amazing pastries makes Macau an easy 
win. And if you want an even deeper dive of Macau, including affordable luxury hotels and the 
best budget eats, we also have a full Macau travel guide with a 3-day budget itinerary on 
our YouTube channel. And now for number one, our ultimate favorite thing to do in Hong Kong 
is indulge in Hong Kong's street food scene. Because in Hong Kong, food isn't just food, it's 
culture. Here are eight classic Hong Kong eats you absolutely need to try. Number one, curry 
fish balls. The OG street snack. Imagine bouncy fish balls bathing in a rich spicy curry sauce. 
Every bite is loaded with flavor and you'll find them literally on every corner for around $2 per 
bowl. Number two, grab a freshly made egg waffle. Crispy on the outside, fluffy on the inside, and 
the plain ones cost around $3 each, but we always go for the chocolate filled ones at $4 from the 
Michelin recommended shop, Mammy Pancake. Number three, if you want something heavier and hardier, 
go for the clay pot rice. Smoky, comforting rice topped with Chinese sausage, tender chicken, 
and chewy bits of octopus. A combo we didn't expect to love this much. We got ours from a 
famous restaurant, Hing Kee, and their dishes cost around $12 each. Our favorite part is that 
toasted crispy rice right at the bottom of the pot. So crunchy and so so good. Number four, the 
pineapple bun. No pineapples here, just a golden crackly sugar crust sitting on top of a soft, 
fluffy bun resembling the shell of a pineapple. It's best when you get a thick slab of cold butter 
inside. For a classic experience, hit Kam Wah Cafe or just grab one from the night markets or local 
bakeries for around $2 to $3 each. Number five, wife cakes. This old school pastry is filled 
with a lightly sweet winter melon paste. Flaky on the outside, chewy on the inside, and not 
overly sweet. Individual pieces usually cost$1 to$2 dollars each and we always grab ours from Ki 
Tsui Cake Shop. Number six, you need to try Hong Kong roast duck or roast goose. Juicy, flavorful, 
and usually hanging proudly in front of the shop, so you know it's legit. Yat Lok is a famous 
Michelin starred spot known for its roasted goose, and you can get a rice meal for about $10. But if 
you're on a tighter budget, head to Cafe De Coral, a popular casual chain with affordable quick rice 
meals and all kinds of yummy Chinese eats. Their roast duck meal is around $7 and it even comes 
with hot tea. Number seven, for a true local breakfast experience, visit the Australia Dairy 
Company, and yes, it has Australia in the name, but trust me, it's a Hong Kong owned institution 
that's been running for over 50 years. The owner just used to work on a dairy farm in Australia. 
Expect silky scrambled eggs, buttery toast, ham macaroni soup, and iced milk tea. This 
full classic Hong Kong breakfast costs about $7 for the whole set. And number eight, last 
but definitely not the least, dim sum. The ultimate Hong Kong comfort food. Whether you love 
hakaw, xiao long bao, lava pao, veggie dumplings, or fried shrimp puffs, which is our personal 
weakness, the variety is endless. Each order usually ranges from $3 to $5 per basket. And 
just a tip, go with a partner or with a group of buddies so you can order everything and share 
them all. For affordable Michelin star dim sum, Tim Ho Wan is always a solid pick. Plus, their 
baked pork buns are to die for. But if you want some spots that the locals adore, try one dim 
sum, dim dim sum, or dim sum here. And yes, they all sound the same, but trust me, you can't 
go wrong with any of them. Hong Kong street food is bold, fun, and honestly part of the whole 
adventure. So, arrive hungry. And by the way, we also filmed a separate food vlog called 
the 15 must try cheap eats in Hong Kong, which hit over 250,000 views. So, if you want the 
exact restaurants and food stalls that we visited, including a map, we've linked that video in the 
description below so you only spend your calories on the good stuff. Well, now that you've seen the 
top 10 most popular tours and activities in Hong Kong, let's talk about the best ways to get around 
the city. Sure, you can always load your Octopus card for commuting on trains, buses, and ferries, 
or you could just take expensive Ubers from point to point all day. But here are a few other budget 
options that might make life easier. Option one, you can take the hop on hop off bus. If you want 
something super easy and flexible, the open top double-decker bus is your best friend. It runs 
across three different routes with 22 convenient bus stops, so you can explore at your own pace 
without stressing about directions. Simply hop on or off at any point on the route, and buses come 
every 45 to 60 minutes. An unlimited pass cost $72 for one day and $99 for 2 days. Option two, 
if you're planning a full day of sightseeing and you're tired of reloading your octopus card, you 
can grab an MTR tourist day pass, which gives you unlimited MTR rides for $10 a day. Just note, it 
does not include city buses, trams, or the airport express. Here are the lines that the pass covers. 
Feel free to screenshot. And lastly, option three, the most efficient way to explore Hong Kong and 
surprisingly affordable if you're traveling with a group. You can book a private car charter. You 
get your own dedicated driver, an 8-hour ride, and full control over your itinerary. And guess 
what? Fuel is already included, and prices go for around $322, good for six people. That's about $54 
per head, making it even cheaper than the hop-on hop-off bus. This is the ultimate budget hack if 
you're traveling with a group. Skip the commuting, skip the stress, and just enjoy your customized 
adventure. Well, now that we've covered the best areas to stay, the best things to do, and the 
best food to eat, it's time for us to put it all together and form the ultimate 5-day budget 
itinerary and cost breakdown. Feel free to save this video for future reference. So, let's 
talk pre-expenses. Before your trip begins, you generally booked your piso sale, your hotels, 
and your eSIMs in advance. Your pre-expenses would probably amount to around $298 per head. For 
day one, welcome to Hong Kong. After landing, activate your eSIM, grab an octopus card at the 
airport counter, and hop on either the airport bus or the airport express straight into the 
city. Once you've checked in and dropped your bags at your hotel, it's time to start strong. 
Head up to Victoria Peak via the Peak Tram and enjoy those insane 360 degree views from the 
Sky Terrace 428. Stay until golden hour for that dramatic Hong Kong postcard moment. Then 
after soaking it all in, head down to Temple Street or Mongkok for some street food dinner. 
Egg waffles, curry fish balls, clay pot rice, or street side dim sum. It's the perfect first 
night feast to kick off your Hong Kong adventure. The total estimated cost for day one is $70 per 
head. Now, day two is all about childhood magic. Hop onto the MTR and make your way to Hong Kong 
Disneyland. Spend the whole day jumping on rides, exploring themed lands, catching the parades, 
snacking on all the cute Disney treats, and staying for the nighttime fireworks. Then end 
your night with a comforting roast goose dinner at Yat Lok before heading back to your hotel. The 
total estimated cost for day two is $119 per head. Now for day three, it's theme park round two, 
but a totally different vibe. Head to Ocean Park for thrilling rides, marine life encounters, 
giant pandas, and cable cars. It's a full day of outdoor fun and rides. Then in the afternoon, 
make your way to the TST promenade. Stroll along the Avenue of Stars for gorgeous night views of 
Victoria Harbour. And if you want that classic Hong Kong moment, ride the Star Ferry from TST to 
Central. It's cheap, quick, and gives you front row skyline views. Then wrap up the night with the 
dinner at Dim Sum Here before heading back to your hotel. The total estimated cost for day three is 
$120 per head. It's day four and time to head to Lantau Island for one of Hong Kong's most peaceful 
experiences. But first, start your morning with a classic Hong Kong breakfast at Australia Dairy 
Company. Order their bestselling silky scrambled eggs, toast, and that comforting ham macaroni soup 
to fuel your day. Then after breakfast, take the MTR to Tung Chung station, which is the jump off 
point for the Ngong ping 360 cable car. And again, a great budget hack is to book the standard cabin 
on the way up and the Crystal Plus cabin on the way down. Once at the top, wander through Ngong 
ping village, climb the steps to the Tian Tan Big Buddha, and explore the serene Po lin monastery. 
Grab a healthy vegetarian meal, browse the village stalls, or simply enjoy the cool mountain 
air. Head back into the city and grab a yummy, budget friendly meal near your hotel and call it 
a night. The total estimated cost for day four is $92 per head. And lastly, day five. For your 
final day, start in West Kowloon at the M+ Museum, home to modern art and digital installations. 
Then stroll over next door to the Hong Kong Palace Museum to see beautifully curated treasures from 
China's dynasties. After that, treat yourself to a good last day lunch. You can go for some classic 
dim sum because you can never go wrong with dim sum. Or if you want one last semi- splurge, 
enjoy a luxury hotel buffet. But of course, make sure to pre-book it online for that sweet 
50% off discount. Seafood towers, sushi stations, roast meats, and endless desserts. Then spend 
the rest of your afternoon shopping. Causeway Bay or Central for fashion and luxury. Mongkok 
or Jordan for quirky snacks and bargain finds. or Dondon Donki, which is Hong Kong's version of Don 
Quijote if you miss Japan style discount shopping. And finally, when it's time to head out, grab 
your luggages from your hotel, take the airport bus or airport express back to the airport. But 
before entering departures, don't forget to refund your Octopus card at the airport arrivals hall to 
get your remaining balance and deposit back. Then board your flight and wave a little goodbye to 
Hong Kong. The total estimated cost for day 5 is $72 per head. If you add all of it together, a 
5-day 4-night Hong Kong adventure would be, drum roll please, $771 per head. This estimated cost 
already covers everything. Flights, hotel, food, transportation, activities, and entrance fees. 
Imagine it already includes major highlights like Disneyland, Ocean Park, Victoria Peak, and even 
that Crystal Plus cabin going to Ngong ping. Think of this itinerary as the ultimate yet practical 
guide for budgetarians who want to experience the very best of Hong Kong without breaking 
the bank. And if you have a few extra days, consider taking a quick side trip to Macau since 
it's just an hour away. And don't forget, we also have a detailed Macau travel guide on our YouTube 
channel if you want all the tips and tricks on how to travel Macau on a budget. On this channel, we 
teach you guys how to save money when traveling, how to book cheap flights, how to book cheap 
hotels, and just a whole lot of travel hacks. So, if this travel guide helped you in any way, please 
hit that like button, hit that subscribe button, and comment down below if you have any questions 
about Hong Kong. We do our best to reply to every single comment, so don't be afraid to ask. We 
hope you guys enjoyed this travel guide, and we hope you enjoy your trip to Hong Kong. This is 
Angelica and Aileen Wanders. Never stop wandering! [Music]

results.title: 11 of the Best Restaurants In Hong Kong (2026) - Paris Eater
results.content:
## Oi Man Sang

One of my most memorable food experiences from Hong Kong was dinner at Oi Man Sang – one of the few dai pai dongs (open-air street food restaurants) left in the city. They use insanely powerful kerosene fuelled wok burners that give an amazing wok hei flavour to the dishes.

The kitchen is literally in the street and it’s well worth watching the wok masters work their magic on the mega powerful burners. The actual tables are more inside in different parts of the adjacent building. [...] #### What to know before you visit

 They’re closed on Sundays
 Hot pot is served for dinner only – they have a special lunch menu which includes curry, chicken rice, noodles etc.
 You can reserve online

Address: G/F, Wo On Building, 9 Wo On Ln, Central, Hong Kong  
Tel: +852 2388 6982  
Website: 

## Sun Hing

Sun Hing is probably my favorite dim sum spot in Hong Kong. One of the few late-night joints open from 3am onwards, it’s a genuine gem of a restaurant in Kennedy Town.

It’s hectic, loud and delicious. Tiny tables are crammed with locals perched on small stools, strangers sitting shoulder to shoulder, barely acknowledging each other, all focused on the food in front of them. [...] #### What to know before you visit

 They’re open daily for lunch and dinner
 Dim sum is only served at lunch
 You can reserve online

Address: 4F, Four Seasons Hotel Hong Kong, 8 Finance St, Central, Hong Kong  
Tel: +852 3196 8882  
Website: 

## Mak’s Noodles

Mak’s Noodles is another great little no-frills noodle shop in Kowloon. Open non-stop from midday to midnight, it’s another great late-night spot for some great food after a night out.

Their menu is pretty diverse with plenty of meat options to go with the homemade noodles and broth. We came for a light lunch so we opted for their signature wonton noodles and shrimp & pork dumpling noodle soup.

* [travel](https://www.pariseater.com/category/travel/)

13/01/2026

# 11 of the Best Restaurants In Hong Kong (2026)

by[Richard](https://www.pariseater.com/author/pariseater_ifwylt/ "View all posts by Richard")

13 minute read

Shares 7

Total

7

Shares

[0](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https://www.pariseater.com/travel/best-restaurants-hong-kong/)

[0](https://x.com/share?&text=11%20of%20the%20Best%20Restaurants%20In%20Hong%20Kong%20%282026%29&via=pariseater_&url=https://www.pariseater.com/travel/best-restaurants-hong-kong/)

[7](https://pinterest.com/pin/create/bookmarklet/?url=https://www.pariseater.com/travel/best-restaurants-hong-kong/&media=https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/best-restaurants-hong-kong-1-1024x683.jpg)

Outside of my hometown of Paris, Hong Kong is easily one of my favorite foodie cities.It’s intense in the best way. The smells, the sounds, the constant buzz – everything revolves around food. It’s a real foodie heaven, and honestly, it’s addictive.

I spent 5 days recently eating my way through the city, tracking down some of the best restaurants in Hong Kong – from insanely good dim sum to Cantonese BBQ roast meats at Michelin-star cafés. Restaurants are listed in the order I visited, and you can find my favorites in the quick restaurant guide below.

**Quick Restaurant Guide**  
  
🥟 **Best Dim Sum in the City**: [Sun Hing](#dimsum)  
  
🪿 **Incredible Michelin Roast Goose**: [Kam’s Roast Goose](#kams) or [Yat Lok](#yatlok)  
  
🍜 **Cantonese Street Food Classics**: [Oi Man Sang](#oiman)  
  
🇭🇰 **Modern Cantonese Food**: [Ho Lee Fook](#holeefook)  
  
🍲 **The Best Hot Pot**: [Big JJ Seafood Hotpot](#hotpot)  
  
🥢 **Delicious Noodles & Wontons**: [Mak Man Kee](#mak) or [Mak’s Noodles](#maksnoodles)

## Kam Fai Dim Sum

*Kam Fai Dim Sum* was our first restaurant visit in Hong Kong after a 12-hour flight from Paris, and it did not disappoint. About a 5 minute walk from Wan Chai MTR station, it’s a genuine where-locals-eat kind of café.

We were shepherded in past the giant outside steamer and sat down by an elderly Chinese Auntie who quickly brings us the menu as well as some tea. This is where we learnt that you need to wash your tea mugs and chopsticks in a plastic bowl with the steaming hot tea.

Our order came quickly – first up was their insanely good signature shrimp dumplings. Sweet, savoury, meaty and incredibly tender – really, really good.

Next up was their char siu rice rolls. These had a lovely texture and were stuffed with chopped-up char siu. The wrappers are a little bland on the outside so you need to use the sauce to really boost the flavour.

Their siu mai are spectacular. Just like their shrimp dumplings, they’re super meaty with a lovely tender texture and seasoned to perfection. Lastly, we also had a plate of steamed chicken and mushrooms which really hit the spot.

#### What to know before you visit

* Open every day from 5:30am to 4pm
* No need to reserve
* They have 2 locations – we went to the Gresson Street restaurant

**Address:** [10 Gresson St, Wan Chai, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/tx8SemsFnWG7cfV98)  
**Tel:** +852 2520 0138

## Ho Lee Fook

*Ho Lee Fook* was our first dinner spot of the trip. It’s a modern, trendy and very vibey Cantonese restaurant in the Central district on Hong Kong island.

Its ultra modern entrance gives way to the impressive open kitchen right next to a very cool staircase leading downstairs to the dining area. It’s a beautiful space – deep red, intricate wallpaper with a mirror ceiling and moody spot lighting.

The food at Ho Lee Fook is modern Cantonese and from what I tried, it’s excellent. We started with some very good clams marinated with, from what I could tell, soy, Chinese cooking wine and finished with chilli and coriander – delicate but delicious. We wanted to try their razor clams with glass noodles but unfortunately they had sold out.

Next up were their awesome dim sum. 4 steamed dumplings and boy were they good. Their siu mai is stuffed with pork, shrimp and scallop – it was super meaty, perfectly seasoned and had a lovely sweetness to it.

For mains we opted for their meat platter. Unfortunately the chicken had sold out which I was really looking forward to but we got extra char siu and goose instead. The char siu is epic – I mean, it’s really, really good. Kurobuta pork from Japan is marinated, roasted and then finished on the charcoal grill. It’s then finished off with their BBQ sauce (which also covers the goose) – it’s a real rice destroyer.

The goose? Very good. Close to the Michelin star cafés featured below. Glassy skin, melty fat and a good amount of bite on the juicy meat.

Finally, I should mention the service is excellent. Our server Britta was fantastic and helped us with food and drink choice.

#### What to know before you visit

* They’re closed on Mondays
* Open for dinner only
* They have a minimum spend of $800 HKD for Thursday-Saturday reservations which is paid as a deposit when you reserve online

**Address:** [3-5 Elgin St, Central, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/1iHAY4rhvP8P22Kb9)  
**Tel:** +852 2810 0860  
**Website:** <https://www.holeefook.com.hk>

## Kam’s Roast Goose

The first of our 1\* Michelin goose was at Kam’s and on our 3rd attempt. This small café in Wan Chai gets busy – really busy so I’d recommend coming early. After 1 hour of queuing at 1:30pm we finally got a table.

There’s maybe 10 tables but the turn-over and service is brisk – pretty typical for HK’s cafés (don’t expect Western Michelin star service!).

We went for an upper-quarter roast goose, char siu and suckling pig with a side of rice. Was it worth the wait? Definitely.

I’ll start with their starred goose which comes with a lovely homemade plum sauce. It was up there with Yat Lok as one of the best we tasted. The skin was perfectly glassy – shattering as you bite into it. There’s a thin layer of subcutaneous melty fat which adds a glorious explosion of flavour.

I really liked their char siu (despite some negative reviews on Google) – it was well charred, juicy and came with a lovely side sauce. Finally their suckling pig is glorious – glassy skin gives way to a thin layer of melt-in-the-mouth meat. I think Kam’s is up there as one of the best roast goose in the city – I really rate it.

#### What to know before you visit

* They’re open every day from 11:30am – 9pm
* Arrive early to avoid long queues – expect to wait at least 1 hour if you go during peak times
* You need to get a ticket from a member of staff at the entrance, you’ll be sat when your number is called. Don’t just queue up!
* They sell out well before close

**Address:** [226 Hennessy Rd, Wan Chai, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/VtKLfjU9rCrfExDBA)  
**Tel:** +852 2520 1110

## Big JJ Seafood Hotpot

*Big JJ Seafood Hotpot* was one of my favorite sit-down evening meals in Hong Kong. It’s just really, really fun with some awesome food, friendly service and great wine.

They offer a modern take on classic Cantonese hot pot with some really well sourced ingredients. Unfortunately our late reservation meant we missed their chicken and bone soups so opted for the 1000 year egg & tomato broth which was super flavoursome.

For those who haven’t tried hot pot before, they bring a selection of sauces, herbs and aromats to build your dipping sauce to your taste.

We opted for 3 different cuts of beef included in the ‘butchers assortment’ and I was blown away by the quality. The marbling is insane – it was almost like Wagyu. The hanger steak slices were *really* beefy and cooked rare (max 5 seconds in the broth) was incredible.

Other than the amazing beef, we tried Japanese oysters that you can just lightly poach in the broth – until they’re just warmed through – creamy and full of flavour. There was also a lovely fish and lamb mixture which you make your own mini-meatballs with – super flavourful. Finally we also had some king oyster mushrooms and bok choy.

I have to mention the wine – their selection is killer and don’t have the ridiculous mark-up that you’ll find in other spots in HK. We had a bottle of their white Big JJ Cuvée (from Burgundy in France) – natural, lively and worked really well with the hot pot.

The vibe at *Big JJ* is incredible – the servers are great fun and all the tables were having a great time. For me, it’s a must-do if you’re in Hong Kong.

#### What to know before you visit

* They’re closed on Sundays
* Hot pot is served for dinner only – they have a special lunch menu which includes curry, chicken rice, noodles etc.
* You can reserve online

**Address:** [G/F, Wo On Building, 9 Wo On Ln, Central, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/AGEu3Vp17noHyCGS7)  
**Tel:** +852 2388 6982  
**Website:** <https://www.bigjjseafood.com>

## Sun Hing

*Sun Hing* is probably my favorite dim sum spot in Hong Kong. One of the few late-night joints open from 3am onwards, it’s a genuine gem of a restaurant in Kennedy Town.

It’s hectic, loud and delicious. Tiny tables are crammed with locals perched on small stools, strangers sitting shoulder to shoulder, barely acknowledging each other, all focused on the food in front of them.

Elderly Chinese Aunties push round trolleys with delicious steamed goodness, dispatching plates to hungry diners. Pretty much no English is spoken here although luckily the menu is translated.

We ordered char siu buns & rice rolls – both delicious although the latter was my favorite. Rich in char siu flavour and perfect with the sauce that our lovely server gesticulated to us that we had to use.

Of course we had to have Siu Mai and shrimp dumplings – both incredibly delicious. Next was their sweet and sour pork ribs – these were bone in small pieces that are fried, glazed and I think re-heated in the steamer. Superb.

Finally we had to have the salted egg yolk buns. These are sweet and so moreish – a must try in Hong Kong.

Sun Hing is the kind of place I love. You go along with it, eat what you’re given, get shouted at but trust that you’re in good hands. As we were leaving a local lady seemed surprised that we were here and I explained that I loved it – she said ‘you’re a local now!’.

#### What to know before you visit

* Open from 3am – 4pm every day
* Cash only
* You’ll be sat whoever and with whoever – don’t expect to have your own table!

**Address:** [8, Markfield Building, Smithfield, Kennedy Town, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/W3FDnh8icjgbWVDYA)  
**Tel:** +852 2816 0616

## Kwan Kee Claypot Rice

*Kwan Kee* is one of the most well-known claypot rice restaurants in the city with queues forming way before opening. Speaking of which, I advise you get there early – even being 10th in the queue as it opened, we were waiting maybe 30 minutes.

It’s like many of the city’s Michelin recognised cafés – hectic, loud yet great fun. You can see the kitchen near the entrance, pumping out food at an amazing rate. The chef carefully juggles dozens of claypots on a huge gas range, meticulously managing each pot’s cuisson.

Their beef and egg rice is superb – the juices from the beef soak into the rice as it cooks and flavours it wonderfully. The egg yolk is barely warmed through which, once broken, flavours the rice even more. What about the bottom? Yup it was perfectly browned and crispy – now that’s difficult to achieve.

We also had their typhoon shelter shrimp which was glorious. Shrimp are deep fried and covered in a deluge of fried garlic, spring onions and chilli. It’s fragrant, spicy and full of umami.

#### What to know before you visit

* They’re open from 5:30-10pm every day
* Arrive early or expect to queue for 1 hour
* They sell out well before close

**Address:** [263 Queen’s Rd W, Sai Ying Pun, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/NdV9mexHHpr47NMP8)  
**Tel:** +852 2803 7209

## Mak Man Kee Noodle Shop

Over in Kowloon you’ll find 1 of 2 very popular noodle shops on Parkes street. Super popular with locals, Man Man Kee is a no-frills noodle and dumpling shop open until late.

The dishes are simple, elegant and come out quick – it’s not a spot you’ll stay a long time in.

I went for the tiger prawn wonton with noodles in soup and it was delicious. The prawn wontons had a great texture, juicy and were perfectly seasoned. The noodles? Springy with a lovely bite. I don’t think I spoke a word as I devoured them. As is the standard in Cantonese cuisine, the broth was very light and delicate.

I’d definitely recommend stopping by here if you’re after something light or a late-night snack – it’s a perfect after-drinks spot. Note that the portions are on the smaller side.

#### What to know before you visit

* They’re open every day from midday until late
* Cash only
* They limit the menu near close

**Address:** [51 Parkes St, Jordan, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/2jCWEqgJVreNvLkJ8)  
**Tel:** +852 2736 5561

## Yat Lok

*Yat Lok* is another extremely popular Michelin starred roast meat café. Similar to Kam’s they’re known mainly for their BBQ roast goose.

We went for an early-ish lunch at 1:30pm on a weekday and there was amazingly no queue. We were ushered in past a huge pot of broth and sat on a shared table in front of a local family. Like the other small family run Michelin approved restaurants, service is *very* brisk.

We ordered an upper-quarter of roast goose, char siu and noodles served in their famous goose broth. Where do I start? Well the char siu was excellent – up there as in the top 3 in the city. It’s thick, super well caramelised and really juicy.

The goose was, not unsurprisingly, excellent, mega crispy skin, nice amount of fat and juicy meat. It had a bite to it and as a local recommended to me, it’s best eaten with your hands. Great flavour and perfect with the house sauce (I think plum) that comes with it.

Finally their noodles in broth were lovely. Super light, delicate yet perfectly seasoned. We were told by another local who was sat next to us that it’s the best in town and that he comes weekly for it.

Yat Lok is definitely worth the hype but I’d add that the Michelin star doesn’t equal Michelin service. It’s very brisk – not something I was bothered about but don’t expect a long drawn out meal. Note that their AC is strong too – even in Winter.

#### What to know before you visit

* They’re open every day from 10:30am to 8:30pm
* Queues can vary – expect a 45 min wait on weekends
* Cash only or expect to pay 30 HKD surcharge for card payments
* They sell out early on in the evening

**Address:** [34-38 Stanley St, Central, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/vmMaEeV94r8XDNVo9)  
**Tel:** +852 2524 3882

## Lung King Heen

*Lung King Heen*, located in the Four Seasons hotel on Hong Kong island is one of eleven two Michelin-star restaurants in the city. It’s very much at a level of European starred restaurants with impeccable service, insane views over to Kowloon and some very refined Cantonese food.

I went with their 6 course tasting menu with the wine pairing. Note that it’s very delicacy focused with not the same emphasis on technique compared to western starred kitchens in Europe.

We kicked off with an amuse bouche and a selection of entrees – char siu, a tempura oyster and suckling pig. The char siu was delicious – hard to fault and the suckling pig had ultra crispy skin with incredibly melt in the mouth meat. The tempura oyster was insanely crunchy but unfortunately the oyster taste was lost with the thick layer of batter.

Next up was a very lovely double boiled crab soup which had a very thick texture – this felt more of a homely dish but it was still delicious.

Probably my favorite dish was stir fried beef fillet with morels. Perfectly glazed, tender beef paired so well with morels and peppers. There was a real depth of flavour to this dish with a lovely background wok hei taste.

Finally there was a forgettable vegetable double boiled soup and a very nice dish of noodles with crab – sweet, savoury and tons of umami.

I took the optional bird-nest soup for dessert which is a real delicacy in China. On its own, it’s rather bland but they serve it with 3 different sauces – a light syrup, coconut milk, and a warm almond cream – which you mix in to taste. Pleasant but it’s more of a delicacy dish than something that blows your mind.

It was a great dinner paired with some excellent European wine (which you pay a big premium for). Service was impeccable and really friendly. A great experience but ultimately, it is eye-wateringly expensive. All in you’re looking at €500+ per person for the tasting menu and wine pairing.

#### What to know before you visit

* They’re open daily for lunch and dinner
* Dim sum is only served at lunch
* You can reserve online

**Address:** [4F, Four Seasons Hotel Hong Kong, 8 Finance St, Central, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/xVGvYkJfT1BJ8peB8)  
**Tel:** +852 3196 8882  
**Website:** <https://www.fourseasons.com/hongkong/dining/restaurants/lung_king_heen/>

## Mak’s Noodles

*Mak’s Noodles* is another great little no-frills noodle shop in Kowloon. Open non-stop from midday to midnight, it’s another great late-night spot for some great food after a night out.

Their menu is pretty diverse with plenty of meat options to go with the homemade noodles and broth. We came for a light lunch so we opted for their signature wonton noodles and shrimp & pork dumpling noodle soup.

Like pretty much every spot we tried dumplings in HK they were perfection. The home made wrapper was the perfect thickness and the fillings were perfectly seasoned. Tender shrimp and juicy pork – they’re addictive.

The noodles had a lovely bite and springy texture and the broth was really flavoursome. Like *Mak Man Kee*, the portions are on the smaller side so think about ordering a second dish if you’re really hungry.

#### What to know before you visit

* They have several restaurants across the city – I tried the below location
* They’re open from 11am to midnight
* Cash only

**Address:** [43 Parkes St, Jordan, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/jogLcx6ULESEjjHd6)  
**Tel:** +852 2302 0908

## Oi Man Sang

One of my most memorable food experiences from Hong Kong was dinner at *Oi Man Sang* – one of the few dai pai dongs (open-air street food restaurants) left in the city. They use insanely powerful kerosene fuelled wok burners that give an amazing wok hei flavour to the dishes.

The kitchen is literally in the street and it’s well worth watching the wok masters work their magic on the mega powerful burners. The actual tables are more inside in different parts of the adjacent building.

We came late which unfortunately meant we missed out on their 2 famous dishes – typhoon shelter crab and beef with potatoes. Luckily they had beef and black pepper sauce with bell peppers and onions. This was just absolutely delicious – the beef was mega caramelised and the sauce had the perfect level of chilli heat. Tons and tons of umami – it was so good I ordered a 2nd dish of it.

Due to the stress of trying to order quickly before the kitchen closed we ended up ordering both razor clams and clams in the same black pepper sauce. Both absolutely delicious and incredibly generous portions.

I absolutely loved Oi Man Sang and I cannot wait to go back. Go with a big group of hungry friends and order plenty of different dishes – it’s so good.

#### What to know before you visit

* They’re open daily from 4:30pm – 11pm
* Go early if you want to try their signature dishes
* The portions are very generous

**Address:** [Sham Shui Po Building, 1A-1C Shek Kip Mei St, Sham Shui Po, Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/XCcQRrtGvaA9N9cr7)  
**Tel:** +852 2393 9315  
**Website:** <https://www.facebook.com/OiManSang>

Total

# Hong Kong Travel Guide 2026 | Best Things To Do (4K)
## Island Hopper TV
522000 subscribers
1075 likes

### Description
67986 views
Posted: 27 Mar 2026
In this Hong Kong travel guide we show you all around this Pearl of the Orient. Hong Kong is famous for its beautiful Victoria Harbour, bronze Buddha on Lantau island, Night markets like Temple Street, Avenue of the Stars in Kowloon, Disneyland and much more. We show you Hong Kong nightlife take the tram up to Victoria Peak, eat some street food and ride the famous MTR, considered one of the best in the world.

This Hong Kong travel vlog shows you around the Hong Kong SAR which is one of several Special Administrative Regions of China. You can do this Hong Kong travel itinerary in about 3 or 4 days. From Hong Kong we end up going to Macau and then over toe Shenzhen. Our Hong Kong 4K video applies to the most up to date information for travel to Hong Kong in 2026.

If you are planning a trip to Hong Kong and looking for the best area to stay we suggest Kowloon for cheaper hotel prices and Hong Kong for more immersive experience around areas like Central, Admiralty or Wan Chai. The main point of this Hong Kong travel vlog is to show you how to spend 3 days or 72 hours in Hong Kong to the max.

to the train station. Well, here it is 6:43 and we caught a ferry. We're going back to the 
area where we began. So that's good luck from the Kaio Fisherman's Village. There was no taxis, 
no bus. So got lucky. We caught this at 6:45 p.m. Well guys, after a long day 
exploring Lantau Island, we're now headed back towards 
Hong Kong on the metro. We could have taken the fast route 
all the way back to Central Station, but we decided to stop at Sim Shasui and 
actually take the water taxi across the Victoria Harbor and get one more look 
at that evening time views. Well guys, we got Cowoon over here, Hong Kong Island 
over here. Another beautiful day in Hong Kong. Well guys, we're back here at 
Victoria Harbor. This time we've got all the lights. Look how beautiful it is. Now, if you're planning to visit Hong Kong and you 
want to do this guide, I would say give yourself 3 to 4 days. We did this in 3 days. I think you 
could easily do it all in three full days. There's another side of the island that we didn't go to. 
That's Stanley and Ocean Park, but because we went to Disneyland, we didn't really feel a need to do 
two theme parks in the video. And Stanley Market, you know, we already did Temple Street. We could 
have also done some of the beaches or hikes in the area, but we didn't show you that. If you're 
interested, you can research the hikes or the beaches. But the big thing that I want you guys to 
know, we're going to Macau. That's going to be an awesome video. Oh, they call that East Las Vegas. 
Then we're going to the Silicon Valley of China, which is Shenzhen, and then on to Guanjo and even 
deeper into China. If you're interested in those, definitely watch our full China series. I'll put 
a link right here. You can click right there.

# Atlas Obscura Itineraries

## Gastro Obscura’s 10 Essential Places to Eat, Drink, and Shop in Hong Kong

Think Hong Kong and the mind flits to dim sum, sweet roasted meats, and noodles. While you’ll still find all of those in abundance here, Hong Kong’s status as a port city and a former British colony have led to dishes that exist outside of the Cantonese genre—indeed, also to entirely new cross-cultural genres.With this in mind, we wanted to put together a list that touches on the classics: a stuck-in-time dim sum hall, a streetside dai pai dong where nearly every dish is cooked in a wok, and an artisanal soy sauce maker. You’ll also find a few lesser-known places that are nonetheless important to Hong Kong’s food scene: the local equivalent of the diner, restaurants that serve Western dishes as perceived through a Chinese lens, Hong Kong’s unique bakeries, as well as things tangential to the territory’s food culture such as kitchen supplies and the neon signs that used to decorate nearly every restaurant in the city.

[View Itinerary](/itineraries/best-restaurants-bars-bakeries-hong-kong)

## Hong Kong Meets America's Post

---

### [**Hong Kong Meets America**](https://www.facebook.com/hkmeetsamerica?__cft__[0]=AZY8hGJmul27u03YS7Jgs5fMMUAL_1ueT6etvTlkwco4xc-EmnqShyf-mhtqT8mM0kGPHyBHIiJOUNO_wHbjneAMPUm4OocpzcMSAHtmZKwedtUZBkzU_6MyT6Cm2aKH_OKN4h1PM8iO7jF-P0TEMjWYX-G3fPa7QCRNVnjT6oh64w&__tn__=-UC%2CP-R)

[November 10, 2025](https://www.facebook.com/hkmeetsamerica/posts/pfbid0bHkvEd9hdrHwVGZuZnujhJoY6GFJxSScSst7Y2qqt1PwbRnwPmkw8H9B4he5CEYLl?__cft__[0]=AZY8hGJmul27u03YS7Jgs5fMMUAL_1ueT6etvTlkwco4xc-EmnqShyf-mhtqT8mM0kGPHyBHIiJOUNO_wHbjneAMPUm4OocpzcMSAHtmZKwedtUZBkzU_6MyT6Cm2aKH_OKN4h1PM8iO7jF-P0TEMjWYX-G3fPa7QCRNVnjT6oh64w&__tn__=%2CO%2CP-R)  ·

Hong Kong has just been named one of Asia’s Best Places to Visit in 2026 by Condé Nast Traveller, celebrated for balancing the ancient with the avant-garde. From world-class bars and sky-high hotels to lush parks and iconic harbour views, Hong Kong has it all.

So what’s making Hong Kong shine even brighter next year? The West Kowloon Cultural District gets a major glow-up with Foster + Partners’ WestK Performing Arts Centre, opening three world-class stages. French culinary legend Daniel Boulud brings Terrace Boulud to Central, adding even more sparkle to the dining scene. The three-runway expansion at Hong Kong International Airport promises smoother, smarter journeys. And for nature lovers, four of Hong Kong's peaks: The Peak, Lantau Peak, Tai Mo Shan and Sai Kung Hoi will have upgraded trails, along with a reimagined Sky100 Observation Deck reopening mid-year.

From high art and haute cuisine to mountain trails and skyline views, 2026 is the year to rediscover Hong Kong in all its glittering contradictions! Read Condé Nast Traveller’s full list of Asia’s Best Places to Visit in 2026 here: [https://www.cntraveller.com/.../best-places-to-go-asia-2026](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.cntraveller.com%2Farticle%2Fbest-places-to-go-asia-2026&h=AUCxxGz4nMO2oIUQFEUW-GgQS3roSMfOOFfhjCEmj0vCmxRDzHmB9XZRBN1-p5vZmd4TInODxfGxEvsNKnVXajRzqkySLcGLq044_77gnpu6qVgSaRtltOnMHQ3r_Xogy1VO8YtwcbRdI9yNjUZOYKdy0u0NiHusvp0_vYUHVWI4Ihuv&__tn__=-UK-R&c[0]=AUBBFXovMqeJbYIgxGnXYaethZ8bNIYQ2Tm3b6aldRBXVdoSZZbRIGKAb2vGNPMkk9RP-gJI5C-NIGQ7EGx_9cQcAzwHL0RouwMKFIu1b1xOK9-XeFGY5dosqNN1JDKpkcGld77iZiasAaEk4X-9M42vMDwjCwpBsN4YsUPVD6haZ013Z8dk_jL8WT0aVOA)
